        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 66746,
          "task_id": 2834492,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4014,
          "example_title": "Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:31:40.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:31 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 78324,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:18",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Express $\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Express $\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k\n\n & = \\bigl( \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) \\bigr) + \\log_4 k \\\\[5pt]\n\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k\\\\[5pt]\n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain\n\\[\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2 k\\big).\n\\]</p>\n<p>Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2 k \\big) & = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 k t t^2\\big)\\\\[5pt]\n\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^{2+1} t^{1+2}\\big)\\\\[5pt]\n\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^3 t^3\\big). \n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Therefore,\n\\[\n\\log_{4}(k^2 t) + \\log_{4}(t^2) + \\log_4(k) = \\log_{4}\\big(k^3 t^3\\big).\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k\n& = \\bigl( \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t\\big) + \\log_{4}\\big(t^2\\big) \\bigr) + \\log_4 k \\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k\\\\[5pt]\n\\end{align}\nUsing the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain\n\\[\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2\\big) + \\log_4 k = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2 k\\big).\n\\]\nFinally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{4}\\big(k^2 t t^2 k \\big) & = \\log_{4}\\big(k^2 k t t^2\\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^{2+1} t^{1+2}\\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{4}\\big(k^3 t^3\\big).\n\\end{align}\nTherefore,\n\\[\n\\log_{4}(k^2 t) + \\log_{4}(t^2) + \\log_4(k) = \\log_{4}\\big(k^3 t^3\\big).\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^3 t^3\\right)$ ",
              "text": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^3 t^3\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\log_{12}\\left(k^2 t + t^2 + k\\right)$  ",
              "text": "$\\log_{12}\\left(k^2 t + t^2 + k\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^2 t + t^2 + k\\right)$  ",
              "text": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^2 t + t^2 + k\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\log_{12}\\left(k^2 t^2\\right)$  ",
              "text": "$\\log_{12}\\left(k^2 t^2\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^2 t^2\\right)$  ",
              "text": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^2 t^2\\right)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^3 t^3\\right)$ ",
            "text": "$\\log_{4}\\left(k^3 t^3\\right)$",

        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 66745,
          "task_id": 2834492,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4014,
          "example_title": "Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:32:03.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:32 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 16705,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:17",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Express $\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)$ as a single logarithm.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Express $\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)$ as a single logarithm.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)\n& = \\bigl( \\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) \\bigr) + \\log_3(pq)\\\\[5pt]\n\n& = \\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)\\\\[5pt]\n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain\n\\[\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)= \\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2 pq\\big).\n\\]</p>\n<p>Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{3} \\big(p^3 q^2 pq\\big)\n& = \\log_{3} \\big(p^3p q^2 q \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{3} \\big(p^{3+1} q^{2+1} \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big).\n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Therefore,\n\\[\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq) = \\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big).\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)\n& = \\bigl( \\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) \\bigr) + \\log_3(pq)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)\\\\[5pt]\n\\end{align}\nUsing the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain\n\\[\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq)= \\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2 pq\\big).\n\\]\nFinally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_{3} \\big(p^3 q^2 pq\\big)\n& = \\log_{3} \\big(p^3p q^2 q \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{3} \\big(p^{3+1} q^{2+1} \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big).\n\\end{align}\nTherefore,\n\\[\n\\log_{3}\\big(p^3\\big) + \\log_{3}\\big(q^2\\big) + \\log_3(pq) = \\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big).\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big)$ ",
              "text": "$\\log_{3}\\big(p^4 q^3\\big)$"
            },

            },

              "html": "$\\log_{3}\\big(p^3 q^2\\big)$  ",

            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\log_{3}\\big(p^3+q^2+pq  \\big)$  ",
              "text": "$\\log_{3}\\big(p^3+q^2+pq \\big)$"
            }
          ],

        {

          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:34:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:34 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 27352,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:27",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Express $\\log(x+2) + \\log(5x)$ as a single logarithm.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Express $\\log(x+2) + \\log(5x)$ as a single logarithm.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms, the given expression can be rewritten as</p>\n<p>\\begin{align}\n\\log(x+2) + \\log(5x) & = \\log\\big( (x+2)\\cdot 5x \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log \\left(5x^2 + 10x \\right).\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule for logarithms, the given expression can be rewritten as\n\\begin{align}\n\\log(x+2) + \\log(5x) & = \\log\\big( (x+2)\\cdot 5x \\big)\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log \\left(5x^2 + 10x \\right).\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 10x \\right)$",
              "text": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 10x \\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 10 \\right)$",
              "text": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 10 \\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\log \\left(6x + 2 \\right)$",
              "text": "$\\log \\left(6x + 2 \\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\log \\left(7x+2 \\right)$",
              "text": "$\\log \\left(7x+2 \\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 5x \\right)$",
              "text": "$\\log \\left(5x^2 + 5x \\right)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

        {
          "number": 3,

          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4015,
          "example_title": "Combining Logarithms With Coefficients Using the Product Rule",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:34:12.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:34 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 93162,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:33",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Express $4\\log 2+4\\log 4 + 4\\log 8$ as a single logarithm.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Express $4\\log 2+4\\log 4 + 4\\log 8$ as a single logarithm.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>If we factor the given expression and use the product rule for logarithms, we get</p>\n<p>\\begin{align}\n4\\log 2+4\\log 4+4 \\log 8\n&= 4 \\left(\\log 2+4\\log 4 + \\log 8\\right) \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4\\log (2 \\cdot 4 \\cdot 8) \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4\\log  64.\n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Therefore,\n\\[\n4\\log 2+4\\log 4 + 4\\log 8 = 4\\log 64.\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nIf we factor the given expression and use the product rule for logarithms, we get\n\\begin{align}\n4\\log 2+4\\log 4+4 \\log 8\n&= 4 \\left(\\log 2+4\\log 4 + \\log 8\\right) \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4\\log (2 \\cdot 4 \\cdot 8) \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4\\log 64.\n\\end{align}\nTherefore,\n\\[\n4\\log 2+4\\log 4 + 4\\log 8 = 4\\log 64.\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": " $4\\log 64$",
              "text": "$4\\log 64$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$4\\log 14$",
              "text": "$4\\log 14$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\log 64$",
              "text": "$\\log 64$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$12\\log 14$",
              "text": "$12\\log 14$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": " $64\\log 64$",
              "text": "$64\\log 64$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": " $4\\log 64$",
            "text": "$4\\log 64$",

          },

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 66757,
          "task_id": 2834492,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4016,
          "example_title": "Separating a Logarithm Into Two Logarithms Using the Product Rule",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:35:21.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:35 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 29684,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:30",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>$\\log_2(8 p) = $</p>\n",
          "question_text": "$\\log_2(8 p) = $",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_2(8 p) = \\log_2 8 + \\log_2 p.\n\\end{align}</p>\n<p>Since $8 = 2^3,$ we have\n\\[\n\\log_2 8 = \\log_2\\left( 2^3 \\right) = 3.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Therefore,\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_2(8 p) & = \\log_2 8 + \\log_2 p\\\\[5pt]\n& = 3 + \\log_2 p.\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_2(8 p) = \\log_2 8 + \\log_2 p.\n\\end{align}\nSince $8 = 2^3,$ we have\n\\[\n\\log_2 8 = \\log_2\\left( 2^3 \\right) = 3.\n\\]\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_2(8 p) & = \\log_2 8 + \\log_2 p\\\\[5pt]\n& = 3 + \\log_2 p.\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$3 + \\log_2 p$ ",
              "text": "$3 + \\log_2 p$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$8 + \\log_2 p$  ",
              "text": "$8 + \\log_2 p$"
            },

              "number": 3,
              "html": "$3\\log_2 p$  ",
              "text": "$3\\log_2 p$"
            },

              "number": 4,
              "html": " $1 + \\log_2(2p)$ ",
              "text": "$1 + \\log_2(2p)$"
            },

              "text": "$3 \\log_2(4p)$"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 47206,
          "task_id": 2834492,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4016,
          "example_title": "Separating a Logarithm Into Two Logarithms Using the Product Rule",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:35:40.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:35 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 16366,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:16",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>$\\log_8(3z)=$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "$\\log_8(3z)=$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\t\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as\n\\begin{align}\n\t\\log_8 (3z ) \n\t\t& = \\log_8 (3 \\cdot z )\\\\[5pt]\n\t\t& = \\log_8 3 + \\log_8 z.\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b = \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as\n\\begin{align}\n\\log_8 (3z )\n& = \\log_8 (3 \\cdot z )\\\\[5pt]\n& = \\log_8 3 + \\log_8 z.\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\log_8 3+\\log_8 z$",

        {
          "number": 7,
          "question_id": 99919,
          "task_id": 2834492,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1473,
          "topic_name": "The Product Rule for Logarithms",
          "example_id": 4018,
          "example_title": "Evaluating an Expression Using the Product Rule ",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:39:02.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:39 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 146903,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:27",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>If $\\log 16 = x$ and $\\log 4=y$, then $2\\log 64=$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "If $\\log 16 = x$ and $\\log 4=y$, then $2\\log 64=$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b= \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule for logarithms and the information given, we get</p>\n<p>\\begin{align}\n2\\log  64 & = 2\\log(16\\cdot 4)\\\\[5pt]\n\n&=2\\bigl( \\log 16 + \\log 4 \\bigr) \\\\[5pt]\n\n& = 2(x + y).\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b= \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule for logarithms and the information given, we get\n\\begin{align}\n2\\log 64 & = 2\\log(16\\cdot 4)\\\\[5pt]\n&=2\\bigl( \\log 16 + \\log 4 \\bigr) \\\\[5pt]\n& = 2(x + y).\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$2(x+y)$",
              "text": "$2(x+y)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$2xy$",
              "text": "$2xy$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$4(x+y)$",
              "text": "$4(x+y)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x+y+2xy$",
              "text": "$x+y+2xy$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$2(x+y)$",
            "text": "$2(x+y)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null

          "time_elapsed_display": "0:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>If $\\ln  x =10$ and $\\ln  y = 2$, then $\\ln (xy)=$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "If $\\ln x =10$ and $\\ln y = 2$, then $\\ln (xy)=$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s recall the product rule for logarithms:</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b= \\log_n(ab)\n\\]</p>\n<p>Using the product rule, we have</p>\n<p>\\begin{align}\n\\ln (xy) & = \\ln x + \\ln y\\\\[5pt]\n\n& = 10 + 2\\\\[5pt]\n& =12.\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:\n\\[\n\\log_n a + \\log_n b= \\log_n(ab)\n\\]\nUsing the product rule, we have\n\\begin{align}\n\\ln (xy) & = \\ln x + \\ln y\\\\[5pt]\n& = 10 + 2\\\\[5pt]\n& =12.\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$12$",
              "text": "$12$"
            },

        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 100636,
          "task_id": 2834592,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1551,
          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4146,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:18:29.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:18 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 29850,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:30",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>If $\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right) = 6$, then $x=$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "If $\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right) = 6$, then $x=$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right) &= 6 \\\\[3pt]\n\te^{\\color{blue}\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right)} &=e^{\\color{blue}6}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln(3-7x)} = 3-7x.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, \n\\begin{align*}\n\t3-7x & = e^6  \\\\[3pt]\n\t- 7x &=  e^6 - 3 \\\\[3pt]\n\tx &= \\dfrac{e^6 - 3}{ - 7} \\\\[3pt]\n\tx &= \\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}.\t\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right) &= 6 \\\\[3pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln \\left( {3-7x} \\right)} &=e^{\\color{blue}6}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln(3-7x)} = 3-7x.$\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align*}\n3-7x & = e^6 \\\\[3pt]\n- 7x &= e^6 - 3 \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{e^6 - 3}{ - 7} \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{3 +e^6}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{3 +e^6}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^7}{6}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^7}{6}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{6 -e^6}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{6 -e^6}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{6+e^7}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{6+e^7}{7}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}$",
            "text": "$\\dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}$",
            "free_response": [],

          },
          "hints": [],

        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 19178,
          "task_id": 2834592,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1551,
          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4146,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:18:43.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:18 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 11068,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:11",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the exact solution to $\n\\ln \\left( {4x} \\right) = 2\n.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the exact solution to $\n\\ln \\left( {4x} \\right) = 2\n.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n   \\ln\\left( {4x} \\right) &= 2 \\\\[3pt]\n    e^{\\color{blue}\\ln\\left( {4x} \\right)} &=e^{\\color{blue}2}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (4x)} = 4x.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, </p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n    4x &= e^{2} \\\\[5pt]\n    x &= \\dfrac{1}{4}e^{2}\\\\[5pt]\n x &= \\dfrac{e^{2}}{4}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln\\left( {4x} \\right) &= 2 \\\\[3pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln\\left( {4x} \\right)} &=e^{\\color{blue}2}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (4x)} = 4x.$\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align*}\n4x &= e^{2} \\\\[5pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{1}{4}e^{2}\\\\[5pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{e^{2}}{4}.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x = \\dfrac{e^2}{4}$",
              "text": "$x = \\dfrac{e^2}{4}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x =\\dfrac{2-e^{2}}{4}$",
              "text": "$x =\\dfrac{2-e^{2}}{4}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x = \\dfrac{{4 - e}}{2}$",
              "text": "$x = \\dfrac{{4 - e}}{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x = \\dfrac{{ {e^4}}}{2}$",
              "text": "$x = \\dfrac{{ {e^4}}}{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x =\\dfrac{1}{2}$",
              "text": "$x =\\dfrac{1}{2}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x = \\dfrac{e^2}{4}$",
            "text": "$x = \\dfrac{e^2}{4}$",
            "free_response": [],

        {
          "number": 3,
          "question_id": 100718,
          "task_id": 2834592,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1551,
          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4147,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:20:44.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:20 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 60821,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:01",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the exact solution to $5\\ln (7-2x) +13 = 38.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the exact solution to $5\\ln (7-2x) +13 = 38.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t5\\ln (7-2x) +13 &= 38 \\\\[3pt]\n\t5\\ln (7-2x) &= 25 \\\\[3pt]\n\t\\ln (7-2x) &= 5\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln{(7-2x)} &= 5 \\\\[3pt]\n\te^{\\color{blue}\\ln{(7-2x)}} &=e^{\\color{blue}5}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (7-2x)} = 7-2x.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, </p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t7-2x  &= e^5 \\\\[5pt]\n\t-2x  &= e^5-7 \\\\[3pt]\n\tx  &= \\dfrac{e^5-7}{-2} \\\\[3pt]\n\tx  &= \\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\n5\\ln (7-2x) +13 &= 38 \\\\[3pt]\n5\\ln (7-2x) &= 25 \\\\[3pt]\n\\ln (7-2x) &= 5\n\\end{align*}\nTo remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln{(7-2x)} &= 5 \\\\[3pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln{(7-2x)}} &=e^{\\color{blue}5}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (7-2x)} = 7-2x.$\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align*}\n7-2x &= e^5 \\\\[5pt]\n-2x &= e^5-7 \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{e^5-7}{-2} \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{7-e^2}{2}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{7-e^2}{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{7+e^5}{5}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{7+e^5}{5}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{7-e^2}{5}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{7-e^2}{5}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{2+e^5}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{2+e^5}{7}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "$\\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}$",
            "text": "$\\dfrac{7-e^5}{2}$",

          "hints": [],

        {
          "number": 4,

          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4147,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:23:00.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:23 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 131480,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:11",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the exact solution to $2\\ln (1-5x) +3 = 9.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the exact solution to $2\\ln (1-5x) +3 = 9.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t2\\ln (1-5x) +3 &= 9 \\\\[3pt]\n\t2\\ln (1-5x) &= 6 \\\\[3pt]\n\t\\ln (1-5x) &= 3\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln{(1-5x)} &= 3 \\\\[3pt]\n\te^{\\color{blue}\\ln{(1-5x)}} &=e^{\\color{blue}3}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (1-5x)} = 1-5x.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, </p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t1-5x  &= e^3 \\\\[5pt]\n\t-5x  &= e^3-1 \\\\[3pt]\n\tx  &= \\dfrac{e^3-1}{-5} \\\\[3pt]\n\tx  &= \\dfrac{1-e^3}{5}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\n2\\ln (1-5x) +3 &= 9 \\\\[3pt]\n2\\ln (1-5x) &= 6 \\\\[3pt]\n\\ln (1-5x) &= 3\n\\end{align*}\nTo remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln{(1-5x)} &= 3 \\\\[3pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln{(1-5x)}} &=e^{\\color{blue}3}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (1-5x)} = 1-5x.$\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align*}\n1-5x &= e^3 \\\\[5pt]\n-5x &= e^3-1 \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{e^3-1}{-5} \\\\[3pt]\nx &= \\dfrac{1-e^3}{5}.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{1-e^3}{5}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{1-e^3}{5}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{e^3+1}{5}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{e^3+1}{5}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{1+e^3}{3}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{1+e^3}{3}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,

              "text": "$\\dfrac{2-e^3}{3}$"
            },

              "text": "$\\dfrac{3+e^3}{5}$"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 110779,
          "task_id": 2834592,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1551,
          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4148,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:28:28.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:28 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 269664,
          "time_elapsed_display": "4:30",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the values of $x$ such that $\\ln(x-1) - 3\\ln(x-1) = 2.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the values of $x$ such that $\\ln(x-1) - 3\\ln(x-1) = 2.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln(x-1) - 3\\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\\\[5pt]\n\t(1-3)\\ln(x-1) &=2 \\\\[5pt]\n\t-2\\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\\\[5pt]\n\t\\ln(x-1) &= -1\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln(x-1) &= -1 \\\\[5pt]\n\te^{\\color{blue}\\ln(x-1)} &= e^{\\color{blue}-1}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (x-1)} = x-1.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, </p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx-1 &= e^{-1} \\\\[5pt]\n\tx-1 &= \\dfrac{1}{e} \\\\[5pt]\n\tx & =  \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln(x-1) - 3\\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\\\[5pt]\n(1-3)\\ln(x-1) &=2 \\\\[5pt]\n-2\\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\\\[5pt]\n\\ln(x-1) &= -1\n\\end{align*}\nTo remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln(x-1) &= -1 \\\\[5pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln(x-1)} &= e^{\\color{blue}-1}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln (x-1)} = x-1.$\nTherefore,\n\\begin{align*}\nx-1 &= e^{-1} \\\\[5pt]\nx-1 &= \\dfrac{1}{e} \\\\[5pt]\nx & = \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1$",
              "text": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$e + 1$",
              "text": "$e + 1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$ e^4 + 1$",
              "text": "$ e^4 + 1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e^4} + 1$",
              "text": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e^4} + 1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\sqrt{e}+1$",
              "text": "$\\sqrt{e}+1$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1$",
            "text": "$ \\dfrac{1}{e} + 1$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [],

            "answer_id": 12890687,
            "select_id": null,
            "question_format": "MC"
          }
        },
        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 70040,
          "task_id": 2834592,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1551,
          "topic_name": "Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm",
          "example_id": 4148,
          "example_title": "Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:29:46.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:29 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 75066,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:15",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>If $2\\ln{x} + 3\\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of  $x?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "If $2\\ln{x} + 3\\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of $x?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t2\\ln{x} + 3\\ln{x} &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n\t(2+3)\\ln x &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n\t5\\ln x &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n\t\\ln x &= \\dfrac{20}{5}\\\\[5pt]\n\t\\ln x &=4.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t\\ln x &= 4 \\\\[5pt]\n\te^{\\color{blue}\\ln x} &= e^{\\color{blue}4}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln x} = x.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, $\tx = e^{4}. $</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\n2\\ln{x} + 3\\ln{x} &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n(2+3)\\ln x &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n5\\ln x &= 20 \\\\[5pt]\n\\ln x &= \\dfrac{20}{5}\\\\[5pt]\n\\ln x &=4.\n\\end{align*}\nTo remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\ln x &= 4 \\\\[5pt]\ne^{\\color{blue}\\ln x} &= e^{\\color{blue}4}\n\\end{align*}\nSince exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\\ln x} = x.$\nTherefore, $ x = e^{4}. $",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$e^4$ ",
              "text": "$e^4$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{e^{2}}{4}$ ",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{e^{2}}{4}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\sqrt e$  ",
              "text": "$\\sqrt e$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\sqrt[3]e$   ",
              "text": "$\\sqrt[3]e$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{e^{20}}{5}$ ",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{e^{20}}{5}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$e^4$ ",
            "text": "$e^4$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },

      "questions": [
        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 24937,
          "task_id": 2831921,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1451,
          "topic_name": "The Average of the Roots Formula",
          "example_id": 14854,
          "example_title": "Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the X-Intercepts",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:12:56.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:12 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 12358,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:12",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>From the graph, we deduce that the roots are $x_1=-5$ and $x_2=1.$ </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx &= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2} \n\t\t= \\dfrac{(-5)+1}{2} \n\t\t= \\dfrac{-4}{2}\n=-2.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "From the graph, we deduce that the roots are $x_1=-5$ and $x_2=1.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx &= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\n= \\dfrac{(-5)+1}{2}\n= \\dfrac{-4}{2}\n=-2.\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x=-2$",
              "text": "$x=-2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x=2$",
              "text": "$x=2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x=1$",
              "text": "$x=1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x=-5$",
              "text": "$x=-5$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x=-1$",
              "text": "$x=-1$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x=-2$",
            "text": "$x=-2$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 462756,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -6.5,\nxmax = 6.5,\nymin = -10.5,\nymax = 2.5,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-10:10,samples=200,color=deepskyblue, line width=1pt]{x^2+4*x-5};\n%\\node[label={0:{$\\color{red}y=x^2-x-20$}}] at (axis cs:3,3) {};\n\n% roots\n\\coordinate [label={below left:{\\small $-5$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-5,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=orangepeel,fill=orangepeel,inner sep=1.5pt] at (A1) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below right:{\\small $1$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:1,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=orangepeel,fill=orangepeel,inner sep=1.5pt] at (A2) {};\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-24937"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 106116,

          "example_title": "Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the X-Intercepts",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:13:33.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:13 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 26509,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:27",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola sketched above.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>From the graph, we deduce that the roots are $x_1=-3$ and $x_2=1.$ </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx &= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2} \n\t\t= \\dfrac{(-3)+1}{2} \n\t\t= \\dfrac{-2}{2}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x= -1.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "From the graph, we deduce that the roots are $x_1=-3$ and $x_2=1.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx &= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\n= \\dfrac{(-3)+1}{2}\n= \\dfrac{-2}{2}.\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore, the axis of symmetry is $x= -1.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x=-1$",
              "text": "$x=-1$"

          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 462767,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -5.5,\nxmax = 3.5,\nymin = -2.5,\nymax = 4.5,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-10:10,samples=200,color=deepskyblue, line width=1pt]{-(x-1)*(x+3)};\n%\\node[label={0:{$\\color{red}y=x^2-x-20$}}] at (axis cs:3,3) {};\n\n% roots\n\\coordinate [label={below right:{\\small $1$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:1,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=orangepeel,fill=orangepeel,inner sep=1.5pt] at (A1) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below left:{\\small $-3$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:-3,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=orangepeel,fill=orangepeel,inner sep=1.5pt] at (A2) {};\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-106116"

        {
          "number": 3,
          "question_id": 106146,
          "task_id": 2831921,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1451,
          "topic_name": "The Average of the Roots Formula",
          "example_id": 14857,
          "example_title": "Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the Parabola’s Equation in Factored Form",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:13:57.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:13 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 8055,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:08",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola $y=(x-2)(x+6).$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the axis of symmetry for the parabola $y=(x-2)(x+6).$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>From the equation $y=(x-2)(x+6),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=2$ and $x_2=-6.$ </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx\t&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n\t\t&= \\dfrac{2+(-6)}{2} \\\\[5pt]\n\t\t&= -2.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "From the equation $y=(x-2)(x+6),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=2$ and $x_2=-6.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx &= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{2+(-6)}{2} \\\\[5pt]\n&= -2.\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-2.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x=-2$",
              "text": "$x=-2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x=-4$",
              "text": "$x=-4$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x=-3$",
              "text": "$x=-3$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x=-\\dfrac32$",
              "text": "$x=-\\dfrac32$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x=-\\dfrac52$",
              "text": "$x=-\\dfrac52$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "$x=-2$",
            "text": "$x=-2$",
            "free_response": [],

        {
          "number": 4,

          "topic_id": 1451,
          "topic_name": "The Average of the Roots Formula",
          "example_id": 14857,
          "example_title": "Finding the Axis of Symmetry From the Parabola’s Equation in Factored Form",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:14:46.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:14 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 46706,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:47",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the axis of symmetry of the parabola $y= (2x-1)\\left(4x+7\\right).$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the axis of symmetry of the parabola $y= (2x-1)\\left(4x+7\\right).$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>From the equation $y=  (2x-1)\\left(4x+7\\right),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=\\dfrac{1}{2}$ and $x_2=-\\dfrac{7}{4}.$  </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx \n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left(\\dfrac12 - \\dfrac74\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left(\\dfrac{2}{4} - \\dfrac74\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left( -\\dfrac54\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=-\\dfrac{5}{8}\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "From the equation $y= (2x-1)\\left(4x+7\\right),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=\\dfrac{1}{2}$ and $x_2=-\\dfrac{7}{4}.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx\n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left(\\dfrac12 - \\dfrac74\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left(\\dfrac{2}{4} - \\dfrac74\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{\\left( -\\dfrac54\\right)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=-\\dfrac{5}{8}\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore, the axis of symmetry is $x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}$",
              "text": "$x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,

              "text": "$x=-\\dfrac{3}{8}$"
            },

              "number": 3,

              "text": "$x=-\\dfrac{7}{8}$"

              "text": "$x=\\dfrac{3}{8}$"
            },

              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x=\\dfrac{5}{4}$",
              "text": "$x=\\dfrac{5}{4}$"
            }
          ],

            "html": "$x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}$",
            "text": "$x=-\\dfrac{5}{8}$",

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 223693,
          "task_id": 2831921,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1451,
          "topic_name": "The Average of the Roots Formula",
          "example_id": 14860,
          "example_title": "Finding an Unknown Value Given the Axis of Symmetry",
          "format": "FR",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:16:14.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:16 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 21855,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:22",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "The axis of symmetry of the parabola $y=(x-5k)(x+7k)$ is $x=4.$ Find the value of $k.$",
          "question_text": "The axis of symmetry of the parabola $y=(x-5k)(x+7k)$ is $x=4.$ Find the value of $k.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4,$ we will set our result equal to $4.$ </p>\n<p>From the equation $y=(x-5k)(x+7k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=5k$ and $x_2=-7k.$ </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx \n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{5k +(-7k)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{-2k}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= -k\n\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>We know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4.$ Therefore, we have\n\\begin{align}\n-k & = 4\\\\[5pt]\n\nk & = \\bbox[4pt,border: 1px solid lightgray]{-4}.\n\\end{align}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4,$ we will set our result equal to $4.$\nFrom the equation $y=(x-5k)(x+7k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=5k$ and $x_2=-7k.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx\n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{5k +(-7k)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{-2k}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= -k\n\\end{align*}\nWe know that the axis of symmetry is $x=4.$ Therefore, we have\n\\begin{align}\n-k & = 4\\\\[5pt]\nk & = \\bbox[4pt,border: 1px solid lightgray]{-4}.\n\\end{align}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": "\\newline\n\n$k=$\n\n[\n    answer:-4;\n    type: number;\n    width: 35;\n    margin-left: 4;\n    margin-right: 1;\n    buttons: frac;\n]",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "\\newline\n$k=$\n[\nanswer:-4;\ntype: number;\nwidth: 35;\nmargin-left: 4;\nmargin-right: 1;\nbuttons: frac;\n]",
          "choices": [],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": null,
            "html": null,
            "text": "-4",
            "free_response": [
              "-4"
            ],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 106180,
          "task_id": 2831921,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1451,
          "topic_name": "The Average of the Roots Formula",
          "example_id": 14860,
          "example_title": "Finding an Unknown Value Given the Axis of Symmetry",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T06:16:47.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 6:16 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 29287,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:29",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>The axis of symmetry of $y=\\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k)$ is $x=1.$ The value of $k=$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "The axis of symmetry of $y=\\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k)$ is $x=1.$ The value of $k=$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1,$ we will set our result equal to $1.$ </p>\n<p>From the equation $y=\\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=-4k$ and $x_2=-3k.$ </p>\n<p>To find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx \n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{(-4k) + (-3k)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{-7k}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=-\\dfrac{7k}{2}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>We know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1.$ Therefore, we have</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\t-\\dfrac{7k}{2} &= 1 \\\\[5pt]\n\tk &=-\\dfrac{2}{7}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we calculate the axis of symmetry in terms of $k.$ Then, since we know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1,$ we will set our result equal to $1.$\nFrom the equation $y=\\dfrac 34(x+4k)(x+3k),$ we find that the roots of the parabola are $x_1=-4k$ and $x_2=-3k.$\nTo find the axis of symmetry, we take the average of the roots, as follows:\n\\begin{align*}\nx\n&= \\dfrac{x_1+x_2}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{(-4k) + (-3k)}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&= \\dfrac{-7k}{2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=-\\dfrac{7k}{2}.\n\\end{align*}\nWe know that the axis of symmetry is $x=1.$ Therefore, we have\n\\begin{align*}\n-\\dfrac{7k}{2} &= 1 \\\\[5pt]\nk &=-\\dfrac{2}{7}.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$-\\dfrac{2}{7}$ ",
              "text": "$-\\dfrac{2}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$-\\dfrac{1}{7}$",
              "text": "$-\\dfrac{1}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$-\\dfrac{3}{7}$",
              "text": "$-\\dfrac{3}{7}$"
            },

              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{3}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{3}{7}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\dfrac{5}{7}$",
              "text": "$\\dfrac{5}{7}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "text": "$-\\dfrac{2}{7}$",

        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 54203,
          "task_id": 2830906,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3220,
          "example_title": "Finding the Center and Radius of a Circle Given Its Equation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T05:55:33.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 5:55 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 8421,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:08",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the center and radius of the circle with the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the center and radius of the circle with the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\n\tr^2 & = 256 \\\\[5pt]\n\tr & = \\sqrt{256} \\\\[5pt]\n\tr & = 16.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.</p>\n<p>Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $16.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nSo, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 256$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\nr^2 & = 256 \\\\[5pt]\nr & = \\sqrt{256} \\\\[5pt]\nr & = 16.\n\\end{align*}\nNote that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.\nTherefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $16.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $16$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $16$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $4$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $4$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $256$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $256$"
            },

              "number": 4,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $512$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $512$"

              "number": 5,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $32$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $32$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $16$",

          "number": 2,

          "example_id": 3220,
          "example_title": "Finding the Center and Radius of a Circle Given Its Equation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T05:55:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 5:55 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 6069,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:06",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 =64 ?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 =64 ?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 64$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\n\tr^2 & = 64 \\\\[5pt]\n\tr &= \\sqrt{64}\\\\[5pt]\n        r &= 8. \n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.</p>\n<p>Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $8.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nSo, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 64$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\nr^2 & = 64 \\\\[5pt]\nr &= \\sqrt{64}\\\\[5pt]\nr &= 8.\n\\end{align*}\nNote that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.\nTherefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $8.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",

        {
          "number": 3,
          "question_id": 116918,
          "task_id": 2830906,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3221,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Graph",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T05:56:07.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 5:56 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 13886,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:14",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the equation of the circle given above?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the equation of the circle given above?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>From the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 13.$ So, the equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 & = 13^2 \\\\[5pt]\n        x^2 + y^2 &= 169.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFrom the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 13.$ So, the equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 13^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 169.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =169$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =169$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{13}$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{13}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 = 13$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 = 13$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$  \\left(x-13\\right)^2 +  \\left(y-13\\right)^2 = \\sqrt{13}$",
              "text": "$ \\left(x-13\\right)^2 + \\left(y-13\\right)^2 = \\sqrt{13}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$  \\left(x-\\sqrt{13}\\right)^2 +  \\left(y-\\sqrt{13}\\right)^2 = 13$",
              "text": "$ \\left(x-\\sqrt{13}\\right)^2 + \\left(y-\\sqrt{13}\\right)^2 = 13$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =169$",
            "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =169$",
            "free_response": [],

          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 401952,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-13$,$13$},\nyticklabels = {$-13$,$13$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below},\nylabel style={above},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-6pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*5pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt},\nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-116918"

        {
          "number": 4,

          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3221,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Graph",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T05:56:20.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 5:56 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 9923,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the equation of the circle given above?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the equation of the circle given above?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>From the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 12.$ So, the equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & =  r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =  12^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =  144.\n\\end{align*} </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFrom the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 12.$ So, the equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 12^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 144.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  144$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 144$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  12$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 12$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  \\sqrt{12}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{12}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  24$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 24$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$(x-12)^2 + (x-12)^2  =  1$ ",
              "text": "$(x-12)^2 + (x-12)^2 = 1$"
            }
          ],

            "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  144$",

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 116921,
          "task_id": 2830906,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3223,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Description",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-13T05:56:36.000Z",
          "created_display": "Mon, Jan 13th @ 5:56 AM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 9951,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 11$ centered at the origin, as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-6562e8a9f12de10b916578928436f50b\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/6562e8a9f12de10b916578928436f50b\" /></div>\n\n\n<p>So, the required equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 11^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 121.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $11$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 11$ centered at the origin, as shown below.\nSo, the required equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 11^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 121.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 121$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 121$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 49$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 49$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 11$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 11$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{11}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{11}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 +11 = 0$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 +11 = 0$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 121$",
            "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 121$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 351479,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-11$,$11$},\nyticklabels = {$-11$,$11$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below},\nylabel style={above},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-6pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*5pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt},\nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $8$ units from the origin?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $8$ units from the origin?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $8$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 8$ centered at the origin, as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-6a19539b449cf86d44c11e17cd2193da\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/6a19539b449cf86d44c11e17cd2193da\" /></div>\n\n\n<p>So, the required equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 &=r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &=8^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &=64.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $8$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 8$ centered at the origin, as shown below.\nSo, the required equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 &=r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &=8^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &=64.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =64$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 =64$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": " $x^2 + y^2 =32$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 =32$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": " $x^2 + y^2 =36$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 =36$"
            },

            },

              "number": 5,
              "html": " $x^2 + y^2 =8$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 =8$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =64$",
            "text": "$x^2 + y^2 =64$",
