        {
          "number": 4,
          "question_id": 180752,
          "task_id": 2829460,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 46,
          "topic_name": "The Constructive Definition of a Set",
          "example_id": 13142,
          "example_title": "Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Over a Finite or Infinite Set",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T22:01:20.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 10:01 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 22102,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:22",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Consider the set $A,$ defined as</p>\n<p>\\[\nA = \\left \\{ \\dfrac{1}{n + 4} : n \\in \\mathbb{N} \\right \\}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Consider the set $A,$ defined as\n\\[\nA = \\left \\{ \\dfrac{1}{n + 4} : n \\in \\mathbb{N} \\right \\}.\n\\]\nWhich of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by</p>\n<p>\\[\na_n = \\dfrac{1}{n + 4}, \\qquad n \\in \\mathbb{N}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Let&#39;s write down the first few terms of our sequence:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\na_1 \n&=\\dfrac{1}{1+4} =\\dfrac{1}{5} \\\\[5pt]\n\na_2 \n&= \\dfrac{1}{2+4} = \\dfrac{1}{6} \\\\[5pt]\n\na_3 \n&=  \\dfrac{1}{3+4} = \\dfrac{1}{7} \\\\[5pt]\n\na_4 \n&=\\dfrac{1}{4+4} = \\dfrac{1}{8}  \\\\[5pt]\n\n&\\vdots \n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, \n\\[\nA = \\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the sequence $a_n,$ given by\n\\[\na_n = \\dfrac{1}{n + 4}, \\qquad n \\in \\mathbb{N}.\n\\]\nLet's write down the first few terms of our sequence:\n\\begin{align*}\na_1\n&=\\dfrac{1}{1+4} =\\dfrac{1}{5} \\\\[5pt]\na_2\n&= \\dfrac{1}{2+4} = \\dfrac{1}{6} \\\\[5pt]\na_3\n&= \\dfrac{1}{3+4} = \\dfrac{1}{7} \\\\[5pt]\na_4\n&=\\dfrac{1}{4+4} = \\dfrac{1}{8} \\\\[5pt]\n&\\vdots\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore,\n\\[\nA = \\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots\\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{8},\\dfrac{1}{9}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{8},\\dfrac{1}{9}, \\ldots\\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{4},\\dfrac{1}{3}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{4},\\dfrac{1}{3}, \\ldots\\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{12},\\dfrac{1}{15}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{12},\\dfrac{1}{15}, \\ldots\\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8} \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8} \\right\\}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
            "text": "$\\left\\{\\dfrac{1}{5},\\dfrac{1}{6},\\dfrac{1}{7},\\dfrac{1}{8}, \\ldots\\right\\}$",
            "free_response": [],

          },
          "hints": [],

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 181711,
          "task_id": 2829460,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 46,
          "topic_name": "The Constructive Definition of a Set",
          "example_id": 13285,
          "example_title": "Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Using a Recursive Sequence",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T22:03:05.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 10:03 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 55396,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:55",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Consider the set $A,$ defined as\n\\[\nA = \\big \\{ a_n\\,:\\,a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n \\,,\\, a_1 = 2,\\, a_2 =4,\\, n \\in\\mathbb N \\big\\}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Which of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Consider the set $A,$ defined as\n\\[\nA = \\big \\{ a_n\\,:\\,a_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n \\,,\\, a_1 = 2,\\, a_2 =4,\\, n \\in\\mathbb N \\big\\}.\n\\]\nWhich of the following sets is equivalent to $A?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the <em>recursive</em> sequence $a_n,$ given by\n\\[\na_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n, \\quad a_1 = 2,\\quad a_2 = 4,\\qquad n \\in\\mathbb N.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Let&#39;s write down the first few terms of our sequence:\n\\begin{align*}\na_1 & = 2\\\\[10pt]\n\na_2 \n&=4 \\\\[10pt]\n\na_3 \n&= a_2 + 2a_1 \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4+2(2)\\\\[5pt]\n&=8 \\\\[10pt]\n\na_4 \n&= a_3+2a_2\\\\[5pt]\n&= 8+2(4)\\\\[5pt]\n&=16 \\\\[10pt]\n\na_5 \n&= a_4+2a_3\\\\[5pt]\n&= 16+2(8)\\\\[5pt]\n&=32 \\\\[10pt]\n\n&\\vdots \n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, \n\\[\nA = \\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The elements of $A$ are all the terms of the recursive sequence $a_n,$ given by\n\\[\na_{n+2} = a_{n+1} + 2a_n, \\quad a_1 = 2,\\quad a_2 = 4,\\qquad n \\in\\mathbb N.\n\\]\nLet's write down the first few terms of our sequence:\n\\begin{align*}\na_1 & = 2\\\\[10pt]\na_2\n&=4 \\\\[10pt]\na_3\n&= a_2 + 2a_1 \\\\[5pt]\n&= 4+2(2)\\\\[5pt]\n&=8 \\\\[10pt]\na_4\n&= a_3+2a_2\\\\[5pt]\n&= 8+2(4)\\\\[5pt]\n&=16 \\\\[10pt]\na_5\n&= a_4+2a_3\\\\[5pt]\n&= 16+2(8)\\\\[5pt]\n&=32 \\\\[10pt]\n&\\vdots\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore,\n\\[\nA = \\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,12,24, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,12,24, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,14,22, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,14,22, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,12,16, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,12,16, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,48, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,48, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
            "text": "$\\left\\{2,4,8,16,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$",

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 181705,
          "task_id": 2829460,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 46,
          "topic_name": "The Constructive Definition of a Set",
          "example_id": 13285,
          "example_title": "Listing the Elements of a Set Defined Using a Recursive Sequence",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T22:07:32.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 10:07 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 264668,
          "time_elapsed_display": "4:25",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Consider the set $B,$ defined as\n\\[\nB = \\big \\{ b_n\\,:\\,b_{n+1} = 3b_{n} \\,,\\, b_1 =2,\\, n \\in \\mathbb N \\big\\}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Which of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Consider the set $B,$ defined as\n\\[\nB = \\big \\{ b_n\\,:\\,b_{n+1} = 3b_{n} \\,,\\, b_1 =2,\\, n \\in \\mathbb N \\big\\}.\n\\]\nWhich of the following sets is equivalent to $B?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The elements of $B$ are all the terms of the <em>recursive</em> sequence $b_n,$ given by\n\\[\nb_{n+1} = 3b_{n} , \\quad b_1 =2,\\qquad n \\in \\mathbb{N}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Let&#39;s write down the first few terms of our sequence:\n\\begin{align*}\nb_1 & = 2 \\\\[10pt]\n\nb_2 \n&= 3b_1\\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(2)\\\\[5pt]\n&= 6 \\\\[10pt]\n\nb_3 \n&= 3b_2 \\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(6)\\\\[5pt]\n&=18 \\\\[10pt]\n\nb_4 \n&= 3b_3\\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(18)\\\\[5pt]\n&=54 \\\\[10pt]\n\n&\\vdots\n\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Therefore, \n\\[\nB = \\left\\{2,6,18,54, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The elements of $B$ are all the terms of the recursive sequence $b_n,$ given by\n\\[\nb_{n+1} = 3b_{n} , \\quad b_1 =2,\\qquad n \\in \\mathbb{N}.\n\\]\nLet's write down the first few terms of our sequence:\n\\begin{align*}\nb_1 & = 2 \\\\[10pt]\nb_2\n&= 3b_1\\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(2)\\\\[5pt]\n&= 6 \\\\[10pt]\nb_3\n&= 3b_2 \\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(6)\\\\[5pt]\n&=18 \\\\[10pt]\nb_4\n&= 3b_3\\\\[5pt]\n&= 3(18)\\\\[5pt]\n&=54 \\\\[10pt]\n&\\vdots\n\\end{align*}\nTherefore,\n\\[\nB = \\left\\{2,6,18,54, \\ldots \\right\\}.\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,54,\\ldots\\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,54,\\ldots\\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18, 24, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18, 24, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,32, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,22,\\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,22,\\ldots \\right\\}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18, 58, \\ldots \\right\\}$",
              "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18, 58, \\ldots \\right\\}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,54,\\ldots\\right\\}$",
            "text": "$\\left\\{2,6,18,54,\\ldots\\right\\}$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",

            "select_id": null,

      ]

    {
      "task_id": 2829602,
      "task_type": "Lesson",
      "topic_id": 118,
      "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
      "course_id": null,

      "completed_at": "2025-01-12T21:46:44.000Z",
      "completed_date": "Sun, Jan 12th, 2025",
      "completed_time": "9:46 PM",
      "started_at": "2025-01-12T20:55:06.000Z",
      "points_possible": 13,
      "points_awarded": 13,
      "progress": 1,
      "progress_display": "100%",
      "review_url": "/learn?taskId=2829602",

      "prerequisites": [
        {
          "id": 1495,
          "name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",

      "question_count": 8,
      "questions": [
        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 70922,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5174,
          "example_title": "Solving a Quadratic Inequality With No Constant Term Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:21:14.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:21 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 93179,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:33",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $x^2+6x \\leq        0?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $x^2+6x \\leq 0?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2+6x&\\leq 0 \\\\\n\tx(x+6) &\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $x(x+6)=0.$ The roots are $x=-6$ and $x=0,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,-6),$ $(-6,0),$ and $(0,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-6)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-6,0)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(0,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+6$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x(x+6)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $x(x+6) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $x(x+6)$ is zero or negative.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-6,0).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-6$ and $x=0.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $x^2+6x  \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [-6,0].$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2+6x&\\leq 0 \\\\\nx(x+6) &\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $x(x+6)=0.$ The roots are $x=-6$ and $x=0,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,-6),$ $(-6,0),$ and $(0,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-6)$\n$(-6,0)$\n$(0,\\infty)$\n$x$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x+6$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x(x+6)$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $x(x+6) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $x(x+6)$ is zero or negative.\nWe see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-6,0).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-6$ and $x=0.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $x^2+6x \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [-6,0].$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in [-6,0]$",
              "text": "$x\\in [-6,0]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty,-6]\\cup [0,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,-6]\\cup [0,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x\\in [0,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in [0,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x\\in [-6,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in [-6,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty,0]$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,0]$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x\\in [-6,0]$",
            "text": "$x\\in [-6,0]$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [],

            "question_format": "MC"
          }
        },
        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 70963,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5174,
          "example_title": "Solving a Quadratic Inequality With No Constant Term Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:22:29.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:22 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 72248,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:12",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-6x \\leq 0?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-6x \\leq 0?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t3x^2-6x&\\leq 0 \\\\\n\t3x(x-2) &\\leq 0.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $3x(x-2)=0.$ The roots are $x=0$ and $x=2,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,0),$ $(0,2),$ and $(2,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,0)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(0,2)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(2,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-2$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$3x(x-2)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $3x(x-2) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $3x(x-2)$ is zero or negative.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(0,2).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=0$ and $x=2.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $3x^2-6x  \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [0,2].$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n3x^2-6x&\\leq 0 \\\\\n3x(x-2) &\\leq 0.\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $3x(x-2)=0.$ The roots are $x=0$ and $x=2,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,0),$ $(0,2),$ and $(2,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,0)$\n$(0,2)$\n$(2,\\infty)$\n$x$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-2$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$3x(x-2)$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $3x(x-2) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $3x(x-2)$ is zero or negative.\nWe see that the expression is negative on the interval $(0,2).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=0$ and $x=2.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $3x^2-6x \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [0,2].$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": " $x\\in [0,2]$",
              "text": "$x\\in [0,2]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "  $x\\in (-\\infty,0] \\cup [2,\\infty) $",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,0] \\cup [2,\\infty) $"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "  $x\\in [-2,0]$",
              "text": "$x\\in [-2,0]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "   $x\\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [0,\\infty) $",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [0,\\infty) $"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "   $x\\in [0,\\infty) $",
              "text": "$x\\in [0,\\infty) $"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": " $x\\in [0,2]$",
            "text": "$x\\in [0,2]$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",

        {
          "number": 3,
          "question_id": 24366,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5175,
          "example_title": "Solving a Quadratic Inequality Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:26:07.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:26 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 130122,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $2x^2-14x+12 \\geq  0?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $2x^2-14x+12 \\geq 0?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t2x^2-14x+12 &\\geq  0 \\\\[3pt]\n\t2(x^2-7x+6) &\\geq  0 \\\\[3pt]\n\t2(x-1)(x-6)  &\\geq  0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $2(x-1)(x-6)=0.$ The roots are $x=1$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,1),$ $(1,6),$ and $(6,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,1)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(1,6)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(6,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-1$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-6$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$2(x-1)(x-6) $</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $2(x-1)(x-6) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $2(x-1)(x-6)$ is zero or positive.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,1)$ and $(6,\\infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=1$ and $x=6.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $2x^2-14x+12 \\geq 0$ for all $x\\in (-\\infty, 1]\\cup[6, \\infty).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n2x^2-14x+12 &\\geq 0 \\\\[3pt]\n2(x^2-7x+6) &\\geq 0 \\\\[3pt]\n2(x-1)(x-6) &\\geq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $2(x-1)(x-6)=0.$ The roots are $x=1$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ intervals $(-\\infty,1),$ $(1,6),$ and $(6,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,1)$\n$(1,6)$\n$(6,\\infty)$\n$x-1$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-6$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$2(x-1)(x-6) $\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $2(x-1)(x-6) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $2(x-1)(x-6)$ is zero or positive.\nWe see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,1)$ and $(6,\\infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=1$ and $x=6.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $2x^2-14x+12 \\geq 0$ for all $x\\in (-\\infty, 1]\\cup[6, \\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",

        {
          "number": 4,
          "question_id": 70919,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5175,
          "example_title": "Solving a Quadratic Inequality Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:28:32.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:28 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 141002,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:21",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $x^2+x-6 \\leq        0?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $x^2+x-6 \\leq 0?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s factor the left-hand side of the inequality:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2+x-6 \t\t&\\leq 0 \\\\\n\t(x+3)(x-2) \t&\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+3)(x-2)=0.$ The roots are $x = -3$ and $x=2,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-3),$ $(-3,2),$ and $(2,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-3)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-3,2)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(2,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+3$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-2$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$(x+3)(x-2)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+3)(x-2) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+3)(x-2)$ is zero or negative.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-3,2).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-3$ and $x=2.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $x^2+x-6  \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [-3,2].$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's factor the left-hand side of the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2+x-6 &\\leq 0 \\\\\n(x+3)(x-2) &\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+3)(x-2)=0.$ The roots are $x = -3$ and $x=2,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-3),$ $(-3,2),$ and $(2,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-3)$\n$(-3,2)$\n$(2,\\infty)$\n$x+3$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-2$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$(x+3)(x-2)$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+3)(x-2) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+3)(x-2)$ is zero or negative.\nWe see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-3,2).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-3$ and $x=2.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $x^2+x-6 \\leq 0$ for all $x\\in [-3,2].$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in [-3,2]$",
              "text": "$x\\in [-3,2]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3] \\cup [2, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3] \\cup [2, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x\\in [2, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in [2, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x\\in [-3, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in [-3, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, 3]$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, 3]$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x\\in [-3,2]$",
            "text": "$x\\in [-3,2]$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 27072,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5176,
          "example_title": "Solving an Inequality by Rearranging Terms and Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:34:51.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:34 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 152251,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:32",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t3x^2-4x-30 &> 5x+24\\\\[3pt]\n\t3x^2-9x-54 &> 0\\\\[3pt]\n\t3(x^2-3x-18) &> 0\\\\[3pt]\n\t3(x+3)(x-6) &> 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $3(x+3)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x=-3$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty, -3),$ $(-3,6),$ and $(6,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-3)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-3,6)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(6,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+3$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-6$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$3(x-6)(x+3)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $3(x-6)(x+3) > 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $3(x-6)(x+3)$ is positive.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty, -3)$ and $(6,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24$ for all  $x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n3x^2-4x-30 &> 5x+24\\\\[3pt]\n3x^2-9x-54 &> 0\\\\[3pt]\n3(x^2-3x-18) &> 0\\\\[3pt]\n3(x+3)(x-6) &> 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $3(x+3)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x=-3$ and $x=6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty, -3),$ $(-3,6),$ and $(6,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-3)$\n$(-3,6)$\n$(6,\\infty)$\n$x+3$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-6$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$3(x-6)(x+3)$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $3(x-6)(x+3) > 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $3(x-6)(x+3)$ is positive.\nWe see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty, -3)$ and $(6,\\infty).$\nTherefore, we conclude that $3x^2-4x-30 > 5x+24$ for all $x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x\\in (-3, 6)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-3, 6)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -6)\\cup(3, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -6)\\cup(3, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x\\in(-\\infty,3)\\cup(6,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in(-\\infty,3)\\cup(6,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x\\in (3,6)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (3,6)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty)$",
            "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -3)\\cup(6, \\infty)$",

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 116194,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5176,
          "example_title": "Solving an Inequality by Rearranging Terms and Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:40:48.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:40 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 354547,
          "time_elapsed_display": "5:55",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the solution of $x^2-x \\geq  4-8x-x^2.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the solution of $x^2-x \\geq 4-8x-x^2.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2-x &\\geq  4-8x-x^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\t2x^2 + 7 x - 4 &\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n    %   2\\left(x^2+\\dfrac{7}{2}x-2\\right)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n       %2(x+4)\\left(x-\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n(x+4)(2x-1) & \\geq 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+4)(2x-1).$ The roots are $x = -4$ and $x=\\dfrac{1}{2},$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-4),$ $\\left(-4,\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right),$ and $\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2},\\infty \\right).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-4)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$\\left(-4,\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2},\\infty \\right)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+4$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$2x-1$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td width=12px align=\"center\">$(x+4)(2x-1)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+4)(2x-1) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+4)(2x-1)$ is zero or positive.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,-4)$ and $\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty\\right).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-4$ and $x=\\dfrac{1}{2}.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $x^2-x \\geq  4-8x-x^2$ for all $x\\in(-\\infty,-4] \\cup \\left[\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty \\right).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2-x &\\geq 4-8x-x^2 \\\\[5pt]\n2x^2 + 7 x - 4 &\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n% 2\\left(x^2+\\dfrac{7}{2}x-2\\right)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n%2(x+4)\\left(x-\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n(x+4)(2x-1) & \\geq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+4)(2x-1).$ The roots are $x = -4$ and $x=\\dfrac{1}{2},$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-4),$ $\\left(-4,\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right),$ and $\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2},\\infty \\right).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-4)$\n$\\left(-4,\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)$\n$\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2},\\infty \\right)$\n$x+4$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$2x-1$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$(x+4)(2x-1)$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+4)(2x-1) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+4)(2x-1)$ is zero or positive.\nWe see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,-4)$ and $\\left(\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty\\right).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-4$ and $x=\\dfrac{1}{2}.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $x^2-x \\geq 4-8x-x^2$ for all $x\\in(-\\infty,-4] \\cup \\left[\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty \\right).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-4] \\cup \\left[\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty \\right)$",

            },

        {
          "number": 7,
          "question_id": 27066,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5176,
          "example_title": "Solving an Inequality by Rearranging Terms and Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:43:28.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:43 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 142556,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:23",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the solution of $\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x \\geq  3.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the solution of $\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x \\geq 3.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x &\\geq  3 \\\\[5pt]\n\t\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x -3 &\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n       \\dfrac{1}{4}(x^2-x-12)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n       \\dfrac{1}{4}(x+3)(x-4)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n(x+3)(x-4)&\\geq 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+3)(x-4).$ The roots are $x = -3$ and $x=4,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-3),$ $(-3,4),$ and $(4,\\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-3)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-3,4)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(4,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+3$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-4$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td width=12px align=\"center\">$(x+3)(x-4)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+3)(x-4) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+3)(x-4)$ is zero or positive.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,-3)$ and $(4, \\infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-3$ and $x=4.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x \\geq  3$ for all $x\\in(-\\infty,-3] \\cup [4, \\infty).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x &\\geq 3 \\\\[5pt]\n\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x -3 &\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n\\dfrac{1}{4}(x^2-x-12)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n\\dfrac{1}{4}(x+3)(x-4)&\\geq 0\\\\[5pt]\n(x+3)(x-4)&\\geq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+3)(x-4).$ The roots are $x = -3$ and $x=4,$ and they split the number line into the $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty,-3),$ $(-3,4),$ and $(4,\\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-3)$\n$(-3,4)$\n$(4,\\infty)$\n$x+3$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-4$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$(x+3)(x-4)$\n$({\\color{red}-})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{blue}+}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{red}-})={\\color{red}-}$\n$({\\color{blue}+})\\cdot({\\color{blue}+})={\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+3)(x-4) \\geq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+3)(x-4)$ is zero or positive.\nWe see that the expression is positive on the intervals $(-\\infty,-3)$ and $(4, \\infty).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-3$ and $x=4.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $\\dfrac{1}{4}x^2-\\dfrac{1}{4}x \\geq 3$ for all $x\\in(-\\infty,-3] \\cup [4, \\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-3]\\cup [4, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-3]\\cup [4, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in [3,4]$",
              "text": "$x \\in [3,4]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x\\in[-3,4]$",
              "text": "$x\\in[-3,4]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-4]\\cup [3, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-4]\\cup [3, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x \\in [-4, -3]$",
              "text": "$x \\in [-4, -3]$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-3]\\cup [4, \\infty)$",
            "text": "$x\\in(-\\infty,-3]\\cup [4, \\infty)$",
            "free_response": [],

          },

        {
          "number": 8,
          "question_id": 86816,
          "task_id": 2829602,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 118,
          "topic_name": "Solving Quadratic Inequalities Using the Sign Table Method",
          "example_id": 5176,
          "example_title": "Solving an Inequality by Rearranging Terms and Using the Sign Table Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T21:46:44.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 9:46 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 192449,
          "time_elapsed_display": "3:12",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>For which values of $x$ is $4x^2-x-10 \\leq 2+3x+3x^{2}?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "For which values of $x$ is $4x^2-x-10 \\leq 2+3x+3x^{2}?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n\t4x^2-x-10 &\\leq 2+3x+3x^{2} \\\\[3pt]\n\tx^2-4x-12 \t&\\leq 0 \\\\[3pt]\n\t(x+2)(x-6)\t&\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Next, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+2)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x = -2$ and $x = 6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty, -2),$ $(-2, 6)$ and $(6, \\infty).$</p>\n<p>Now, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:</p>\n<table rules=all cellpadding=10 align=\"center\" style=\"border:1px solid black; margin: auto; margin-bottom: 4mm; margin-top: 4mm\">\n<tr>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\"></td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-\\infty,-2)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(-2,6)$</td>\n    <td width=20px bgcolor=\"#D6EAF8\" align=\"center\" style=\"font-weight:bold\">$(6,\\infty)$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x+2$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$x-6$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n    <td width=12px align=\"center\">$(x-6)(x+2)$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n    <td width=60px align=\"center\">${\\color{red}-}$</td>\n    <td width=40px align=\"center\">${\\color{blue}+}$</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n\n<p>Finally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+2)(x-6) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+2)(x-6)$ is zero or negative.</p>\n<p>We see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-2,6).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-2$ and $x=6.$</p>\n<p>Therefore, we conclude that $4x^2-x-10 \\leq 2+3x+3x^{2}$ for all $x \\in [-2, 6].$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, we rewrite the given inequality so that the right-hand side equals zero. Then, we factor the left-hand side:\n\\begin{align*}\n4x^2-x-10 &\\leq 2+3x+3x^{2} \\\\[3pt]\nx^2-4x-12 &\\leq 0 \\\\[3pt]\n(x+2)(x-6) &\\leq 0\n\\end{align*}\nNext, we find the roots of the corresponding equation $(x+2)(x-6) = 0.$ The roots are $x = -2$ and $x = 6,$ and they split the number line into $3$ subintervals $(-\\infty, -2),$ $(-2, 6)$ and $(6, \\infty).$\nNow, we construct a sign table with the list of the intervals on the top and the list of factors on the left. Filling in the sign table using the usual method, we get the following result:\n$(-\\infty,-2)$\n$(-2,6)$\n$(6,\\infty)$\n$x+2$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$x-6$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n$(x-6)(x+2)$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\n${\\color{red}-}$\n${\\color{blue}+}$\nFinally, to solve the quadratic inequality $(x+2)(x-6) \\leq 0,$ we look at the bottom row to find out where the expression $(x+2)(x-6)$ is zero or negative.\nWe see that the expression is negative on the interval $(-2,6).$ Also, we know that the expression equals $0$ at the points $x=-2$ and $x=6.$\nTherefore, we conclude that $4x^2-x-10 \\leq 2+3x+3x^{2}$ for all $x \\in [-2, 6].$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in [-2, 6]$ ",
              "text": "$x\\in [-2, 6]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, 2] \\cup [6,\\infty)$  ",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, 2] \\cup [6,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -2] \\cup [6,\\infty)$  ",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty, -2] \\cup [6,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x\\in [2, 6]$  ",
              "text": "$x\\in [2, 6]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x\\in [-6, 2]$  ",
              "text": "$x\\in [-6, 2]$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x\\in [-2, 6]$ ",
            "text": "$x\\in [-2, 6]$",

      "question_count": 5,
      "questions": [
        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 125572,
          "task_id": 2828332,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1167,
          "topic_name": "Index Notation for Matrices",
          "example_id": 4371,
          "example_title": "Finding an Entry of a Matrix",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:53:08.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:53 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 5413,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:05",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the entry $h_{22}$ of the matrix ${H}$, where \n\\[{H}\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & 0 & -5 \\\\\n3 & 4 & 2 \\\\\n-2 & 5 & 4 \\\\\n7 & -4 & 6\n\\end{bmatrix}.\\] </p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the entry $h_{22}$ of the matrix ${H}$, where\n\\[{H}\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & 0 & -5 \\\\\n3 & 4 & 2 \\\\\n-2 & 5 & 4 \\\\\n7 & -4 & 6\n\\end{bmatrix}.\\]",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The entry $h_{22}$ should lie at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $2$nd column. Therefore, $h_{22}=\\color{blue}{4}.$\n\\[\n{H}\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & 0 & -5 \\\\\n3 & \\color{blue}4 & 2 \\\\\n-2 & 5 & 4 \\\\\n7 & -4 & 6\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\] </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The entry $h_{22}$ should lie at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $2$nd column. Therefore, $h_{22}=\\color{blue}{4}.$\n\\[\n{H}\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & 0 & -5 \\\\\n3 & \\color{blue}4 & 2 \\\\\n-2 & 5 & 4 \\\\\n7 & -4 & 6\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$4$",
              "text": "$4$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$-2$",
              "text": "$-2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$5$",
              "text": "$5$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$2$",
              "text": "$2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "No such entry",
              "text": "No such entry"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$4$",
            "text": "$4$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },

        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 125593,
          "task_id": 2828332,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1167,
          "topic_name": "Index Notation for Matrices",
          "example_id": 4372,
          "example_title": "Finding an Entry of a Square Matrix",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:53:36.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:53 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 8721,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:09",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the entry $r_{43}$ of the matrix $R$, where \n\\[\nR\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n-5 & 2 & \\sqrt{3}  & 4\\\\\n-3 & 0 & -4 & 5\\\\\n5 & -2 & 8 & -1\\\\\n0 & -1 & 1 & \\sqrt{2}\n\\end{bmatrix}.\n\\] </p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the entry $r_{43}$ of the matrix $R$, where\n\\[\nR\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n-5 & 2 & \\sqrt{3} & 4\\\\\n-3 & 0 & -4 & 5\\\\\n5 & -2 & 8 & -1\\\\\n0 & -1 & 1 & \\sqrt{2}\n\\end{bmatrix}.\n\\]",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The entry $r_{43}$ lies at the intersection of the $4$th row and $3$rd column. Therefore, $r_{43}={\\color{blue}1}.$\n\\[\nE\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n-5 & 2 & \\sqrt{3}  & 4\\\\\n-3 & 0 & -4 & 5\\\\\n5 & -2 & 8 & -1\\\\\n0 & -1 & \\color{blue}1 & \\sqrt{2}\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\] </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The entry $r_{43}$ lies at the intersection of the $4$th row and $3$rd column. Therefore, $r_{43}={\\color{blue}1}.$\n\\[\nE\n=\\begin{bmatrix}\n-5 & 2 & \\sqrt{3} & 4\\\\\n-3 & 0 & -4 & 5\\\\\n5 & -2 & 8 & -1\\\\\n0 & -1 & \\color{blue}1 & \\sqrt{2}\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [

              "number": 1,
              "html": "$1$",
              "text": "$1$"

              "number": 2,

            },

              "text": "No such entry"

          ],

        {
          "number": 3,

          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:53:52.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:53 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 7139,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:07",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Find the entry $t_{23}$ of the matrix ${T}$, where \n\\[\nT = \n\\begin{bmatrix}   \n\t1 & -2 & 3 \\\\\n\t5 & 2 & -1\\\\\n\t0 & 8 & 7\n\\end{bmatrix}.\n\\]</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Find the entry $t_{23}$ of the matrix ${T}$, where\n\\[\nT =\n\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & -2 & 3 \\\\\n5 & 2 & -1\\\\\n0 & 8 & 7\n\\end{bmatrix}.\n\\]",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The entry $t_{23}$ lies at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $3$rd column. Therefore, $t_{23}={\\color{blue}-1}.$\n\\[\nT = \n\\begin{bmatrix}   \n\t1 & -2 & 3 \\\\\n\t5 & 2 & \\color{blue}-1\\\\\n\t0 & 8 & 7\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The entry $t_{23}$ lies at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $3$rd column. Therefore, $t_{23}={\\color{blue}-1}.$\n\\[\nT =\n\\begin{bmatrix}\n1 & -2 & 3 \\\\\n5 & 2 & \\color{blue}-1\\\\\n0 & 8 & 7\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$-1$",
              "text": "$-1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,

            },

              "number": 3,

        {
          "number": 4,
          "question_id": 13502,
          "task_id": 2828332,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1167,
          "topic_name": "Index Notation for Matrices",
          "example_id": 4373,
          "example_title": "Describing an Entry of a Matrix in Index Notation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:54:33.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:54 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 29942,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:30",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Consider the highlighted entry of the matrix $F$ given below. Describe it in terms of $f_{ij}.$</p>\n<p>\\[\nF = \n\\begin{bmatrix}   \n\t3 & 5 & 10 & 6 \\\\\n\t6 & {\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{14}} & 8 & 1\\\\\n\t9 & 17 & 9 & 0\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Consider the highlighted entry of the matrix $F$ given below. Describe it in terms of $f_{ij}.$\n\\[\nF =\n\\begin{bmatrix}\n3 & 5 & 10 & 6 \\\\\n6 & {\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{14}} & 8 & 1\\\\\n9 & 17 & 9 & 0\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The entry lies at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $2$nd column. Hence, we have $f_{22}={\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{14}}.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The entry lies at the intersection of the $2$nd row and $2$nd column. Hence, we have $f_{22}={\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{14}}.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 13499,
          "task_id": 2828332,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1167,
          "topic_name": "Index Notation for Matrices",
          "example_id": 4373,
          "example_title": "Describing an Entry of a Matrix in Index Notation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:54:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:54 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 6790,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:07",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Consider the highlighted entry of the matrix $A$ given below. Describe it in terms of $a_{ij}.$</p>\n<p>\\[\nA = \n\\begin{bmatrix}   \n\t3 & 5 & 10 & 6 \\\\\n\t6 & 14 & 8 & 1\\\\\n\t9 & 17 & 9 & {\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{0}}\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Consider the highlighted entry of the matrix $A$ given below. Describe it in terms of $a_{ij}.$\n\\[\nA =\n\\begin{bmatrix}\n3 & 5 & 10 & 6 \\\\\n6 & 14 & 8 & 1\\\\\n9 & 17 & 9 & {\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{0}}\n\\end{bmatrix}\n\\]",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The entry lies at the intersection of the $3$rd row and $4$th column. Hence, we have $a_{34}={\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{0}}.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The entry lies at the intersection of the $3$rd row and $4$th column. Hence, we have $a_{34}={\\bbox[lightgray, 2pt]{0}}.$",

      "task_id": 2829302,

      "questions": [
        {
          "number": 1,
          "question_id": 116909,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3220,
          "example_title": "Finding the Center and Radius of a Circle Given Its Equation",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:40:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:40 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 9439,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:09",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 6 ?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the center and the radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 6 ?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\n\tx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 =6$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\n\tr^2\t&= 6 \\\\[5pt]\n\tr\t&= \\sqrt{6} .\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.</p>\n<p>Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $\\sqrt{6}.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nSo, the equation $x^2 + y^2 =6$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\nr^2 &= 6 \\\\[5pt]\nr &= \\sqrt{6} .\n\\end{align*}\nNote that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.\nTherefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $\\sqrt{6}.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $6$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $6$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $36$",
              "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $36$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "The center is $(6,6)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$",
              "text": "The center is $(6,6)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "The center is $(6,6)$ and the radius is $6$",
              "text": "The center is $(6,6)$ and the radius is $6$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$",
            "text": "The center is $(0,0)$ and the radius is $\\sqrt{6}$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",

          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the center and radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 7?$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the center and radius of the circle with equation $x^2 + y^2 = 7?$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 7$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\n\tr^2 & = 7 \\\\[5pt]\n\tr & = \\sqrt{7} .\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Note that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.</p>\n<p>Therefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is  $\\sqrt{7}.$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nSo, the equation $x^2 + y^2 = 7$ represents a circle that is centered at the origin, with radius\n\\begin{align*}\nr^2 & = 7 \\\\[5pt]\nr & = \\sqrt{7} .\n\\end{align*}\nNote that we disregarded the negative solution for the radius since the radius of a circle must be positive.\nTherefore, the circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and its radius is $\\sqrt{7}.$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",

        {
          "number": 3,

          "task_id": 2829302,

          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3221,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Graph",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:41:23.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:41 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 12250,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:12",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the equation of the circle given above?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the equation of the circle given above?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>From the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 14.$ So, the equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 & =14^2 \\\\[5pt]\n        x^2 + y^2 &= 196.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFrom the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 14.$ So, the equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =14^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 196.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =196$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =196$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{14}$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{14}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 = 14$",
              "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 = 14$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$  \\left(x-14\\right)^2 +  \\left(y-14\\right)^2 = \\sqrt{14}$",
              "text": "$ \\left(x-14\\right)^2 + \\left(y-14\\right)^2 = \\sqrt{14}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$  \\left(x-\\sqrt{14}\\right)^2 +  \\left(y-\\sqrt{14}\\right)^2 = 14$",
              "text": "$ \\left(x-\\sqrt{14}\\right)^2 + \\left(y-\\sqrt{14}\\right)^2 = 14$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 2,
            "html": "$  x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{14}$",
            "text": "$ x^2 + y^2 =\\sqrt{14}$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 401953,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-14$,$14$},\nyticklabels = {$-14$,$14$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-6pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*5pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt},\nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-116919"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 4,

          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3221,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Graph",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:41:45.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:41 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 13893,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:14",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the equation of the circle given above?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the equation of the circle given above?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>From the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = \\sqrt{2}.$ So, the equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 & = \\left(\\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 \\\\[5pt]\n        x^2 + y^2 &= 2.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFrom the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = \\sqrt{2}.$ So, the equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = \\left(\\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 2.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 2$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{2}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 4$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 4$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$\\left(x- \\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 + \\left(y- \\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 =2$",
              "text": "$\\left(x- \\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 + \\left(y- \\sqrt{2}\\right)^2 =2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$\\left(x- 2\\right)^2 + \\left(y-2\\right)^2 =2$",
              "text": "$\\left(x- 2\\right)^2 + \\left(y-2\\right)^2 =2$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 2$",
            "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 2$",
            "free_response": [],
