          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 401948,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.5,\nxmax = 1.5,\nymin = -1.5,\nymax = 1.5,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-\\sqrt2$,$\\sqrt2$},\nytick = {-1,1},\nyticklabels = {$-\\sqrt2$,$\\sqrt2$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-12pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*7pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-6pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=2pt}, \nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({sin(x)},{cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-116914"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 54191,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3221,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Graph",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:41:58.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:41 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 10412,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>What is the equation of the circle given above?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "What is the equation of the circle given above?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>From the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 9.$ So, the equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 9^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 81.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The general equation of a circle with radius $r$ centered at the origin is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFrom the picture, we see that our circle is centered at $(0,0)$ and has radius $r = 9.$ So, the equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 9^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 81.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  = 81$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 81$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  = 9$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 9$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  = 3$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 3$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  = 18$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 18$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$(x-9)^2 + (y-9)^2  = 1$ ",
              "text": "$(x-9)^2 + (y-9)^2 = 1$"
            }
          ],

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 116922,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3223,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Description",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:42:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:42 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 37793,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:38",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 13$ centered at the origin, as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-dbf128917dbe9b51852dd2ebf7a0d3d2\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/dbf128917dbe9b51852dd2ebf7a0d3d2\" /></div>\n\n\n<p>So, the required equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 13^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 169.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $13$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 13$ centered at the origin, as shown below.\nSo, the required equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 13^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 169.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 169$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 169$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 +169 = 0$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 +169 = 0$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 13$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 13$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{13}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{13}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 +13 = 0$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 +13 = 0$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 169$",
            "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 169$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 351480,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-13$,$13$},\nyticklabels = {$-13$,$13$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-7pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt}, \nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

              "id": 401956,

        {
          "number": 7,
          "question_id": 116923,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3223,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given a Description",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:42:55.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:42 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 10174,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:10",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $15$ units from the origin?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Which equation describes the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the coordinate plane that are a distance of $15$ units from the origin?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $15$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 15$ centered at the origin, as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-a3689bd4a9828cf75b524d9aa4a082ba\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/a3689bd4a9828cf75b524d9aa4a082ba\" /></div>\n\n\n<p>So, the required equation is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 15^2 \\\\[5pt]\n\tx^2 + y^2 &= 225.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The set of all points $(x,y)$ that are a distance of $15$ units from the origin defines a circle with radius $r = 15$ centered at the origin, as shown below.\nSo, the required equation is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 &= r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 15^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 &= 225.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 225$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 225$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 + 225 =0$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 + 225 =0$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 15$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 15$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{15}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{15}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 +15 = 0$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 +15 = 0$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "free_response": [],

          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 351481,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-15$,$15$},\nyticklabels = {$-15$,$15$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-7pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt}, \nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

            {

              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows}\n\\usetikzlibrary{intersections}\n\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween}\n\n\\definecolor{verylightgray}{RGB}{242, 242, 242}\n\\definecolor{deepskyblue}{rgb}{0.173,0.447,0.682}\n\\definecolor{orangepeel}{rgb}{0.902, 0.624, 0}\n\\definecolor{lightyellow}{rgb}{0.953,0.882,0.388}\n\\definecolor{darkgreen}{rgb}{0.271, 0.608, 0.463}\n\\definecolor{lightblue}{rgb}{0.484,0.702,0.894}\n\\definecolor{rustred}{rgb}{0.784,0.392,0.149}\n\\definecolor{fadedpink}{rgb}{0.753,0.494,0.643}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\pagestyle{empty}\n\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\n\\begin{axis}[\ntrig format plots=rad,\nlegend pos=outer north east,\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize}, \nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.4,\nxmax = 1.4,\nymin = -1.4,\nymax = 1.4,\nxtick = {-1,1},\nytick = {-1,1},\nxticklabels = {$-15$,$15$},\nyticklabels = {$-15$,$15$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-10pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n\t\t\t    yshift=2pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-7pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt}, \n\t\t\t    xshift=3pt}, \nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, very thick]({1*sin(x)},{1*cos(x)});\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-116923-e-0"

          "raw": {

        {
          "number": 8,
          "question_id": 14911,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3225,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:46:01.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:46 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 97007,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:37",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,5)$ and $(-6,-5),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,5)$ and $(-6,-5),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{AB}\\mathbin{:}$\n\\[ \\begin{align*}\nAB&=\\sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(6-(-6))}^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{10}^2+12^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{244}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{4\\cdot 61}\\\\[5pt]\n&=2\\sqrt{61}\n\\end{align*}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Since $\\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is\n\\[\nr = \\dfrac{AB}{2}=\\dfrac {2\\sqrt{61}}{2}=\\sqrt{61}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation of the circle is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & =  r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = (\\sqrt{61})^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =  61.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFirst, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{AB}\\mathbin{:}$\n\\[ \\begin{align*}\nAB&=\\sqrt{{(5-(-5))}^2+{(6-(-6))}^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{10}^2+12^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{244}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{4\\cdot 61}\\\\[5pt]\n&=2\\sqrt{61}\n\\end{align*}\n\\]\nSince $\\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is\n\\[\nr = \\dfrac{AB}{2}=\\dfrac {2\\sqrt{61}}{2}=\\sqrt{61}.\n\\]\nSo, the equation of the circle is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = (\\sqrt{61})^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 61.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  61$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 61$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  (61)^2$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = (61)^2$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  \\sqrt{61}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{61}$"
            },

              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  = 122$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 122$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  244$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 244$"
            }

thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\nxmin = -1.5,\nxmax = 1.5,\nymin = -1.5,\nymax = 1.5,\nxtick = {0},\n%xticklabels = {$-\\sqrt2$,$\\sqrt2$},\nytick = {0},\n%yticklabels = {$-\\sqrt2$,$\\sqrt2$},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above right},\n%% Here, we can control the positioning of each individual tick.\nx tick label style={xshift={(\\tick==-1)*-11pt},\n\t\t\t     xshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n                yshift=3pt},\ny tick label style={yshift={(\\tick==-1)*-6pt},\n\t\t\t     yshift={(\\tick==1)*6pt},\n                    xshift=2pt},\nevery axis x label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=west,\n},\nevery axis y label/.style={\n\tat={(ticklabel* cs:1.02)},\n\tanchor=south,\n}\n]\n\n\n\\def\\Ax{6}\n\\def\\Ay{5}\n\\pgfmathtruncatemacro\\Bx{-\\Ax}\n\\pgfmathtruncatemacro\\By{-\\Ay}\n\n\\pgfmathsetmacro\\R{sqrt((\\Ax)^2+(\\Ay)^2)}\n\n\\def\\lcolor{deepskyblue};\n\\def\\pcolor{orangepeel};\n\n% Diameter\n\\draw[dashed, \\pcolor, line width=0.8pt] (axis cs:{\\Ax/\\R},{\\Ay/\\R}) -- (axis cs:{\\Bx/\\R},{\\By/\\R});\n\n% Circle\n\\addplot [domain=0:2*pi, samples=200, color=\\lcolor, line {};\n\\node[circle,color=deepskyblue,fill,inner sep=1.8pt, label={180,xshift=2pt,yshift=-3pt:{$(\\Bx,\\By)$}}] at (axis cs:{\\Bx/\\R},{\\By/\\R})         {
          "number": 9,
          "question_id": 14903,

          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3225,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:47:22.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:47 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 71474,
          "time_elapsed_display": "1:11",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,1)$ and $(-6,-1),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Given that the points $A$ and $B$ with coordinates $(6,1)$ and $(-6,-1),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{AB}\\mathbin{:}$</p>\n<p>\\[ \\begin{align*}\nAB&=\\sqrt{{(6-(-6))}^2+{(1-(-1))}^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{12}^2+2^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{148}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{4 \\cdot 37}\\\\[5pt]\n&=2\\sqrt{37}\n\\end{align*}\n\\]\nSince $\\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is</p>\n<p> \\[r=\\dfrac{AB}{2} =\\dfrac {2\\sqrt{37}}{2}=\\sqrt{37}.\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation of the circle is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & =  r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = {\\left(\\sqrt{37}\\right)}^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =  37.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFirst, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{AB}\\mathbin{:}$\n\\[ \\begin{align*}\nAB&=\\sqrt{{(6-(-6))}^2+{(1-(-1))}^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{12}^2+2^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{148}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{4 \\cdot 37}\\\\[5pt]\n&=2\\sqrt{37}\n\\end{align*}\n\\]\nSince $\\overline{AB}$ is a diameter, the radius is\n\\[r=\\dfrac{AB}{2} =\\dfrac {2\\sqrt{37}}{2}=\\sqrt{37}.\\]\nSo, the equation of the circle is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = {\\left(\\sqrt{37}\\right)}^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 37.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  37$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 37$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  \\sqrt{37}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = \\sqrt{37}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  (37)^2$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = (37)^2$"
            },

              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  148$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 148$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$(x-4)^2 + (y-1)^2  =  37$",
              "text": "$(x-4)^2 + (y-1)^2 = 37$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "$x^2 + y^2  =  148$",

        {
          "number": 10,
          "question_id": 14912,
          "task_id": 2829302,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 842,
          "topic_name": "Equations of Circles Centered at the Origin",
          "example_id": 3225,
          "example_title": "Finding the Equation of a Circle Given Endpoints of a Diameter",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:50:23.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:50 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 164230,
          "time_elapsed_display": "2:44",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(3,3)$ and $(-3,-3),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Given that the points $X$ and $Y$ with coordinates $(3,3)$ and $(-3,-3),$ respectively, are the endpoints of a diameter of a circle centered at the origin, what is the equation of the circle?",
          "explanation_html": "<p>The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is \n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>First, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{XY}\\mathbin{:}$</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nXY &=\\sqrt{{(3-(-3))}^2+{(3-(-3))}^2} \\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{6}^2+6^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{36+36}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{72}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{2\\cdot 36}\\\\[5pt]\n&=6\\sqrt2\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Since $\\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is</p>\n<p>\\[\nr=\\dfrac{XY}{2}=\\dfrac {6\\sqrt2}{2}=3\\sqrt2.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the equation of the circle is</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & =  r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = \\left({3\\sqrt2}\\right)^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & =  18.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "The equation of a circle with radius $r,$ centered at the origin, is\n\\[\nx^2 + y^2 = r^2.\n\\]\nFirst, we calculate the length of the diameter $\\overline{XY}\\mathbin{:}$\n\\begin{align*}\nXY &=\\sqrt{{(3-(-3))}^2+{(3-(-3))}^2} \\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{{6}^2+6^2}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{36+36}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{72}\\\\[5pt]\n&=\\sqrt{2\\cdot 36}\\\\[5pt]\n&=6\\sqrt2\n\\end{align*}\nSince $\\overline{XY}$ is a diameter, the radius is\n\\[\nr=\\dfrac{XY}{2}=\\dfrac {6\\sqrt2}{2}=3\\sqrt2.\n\\]\nSo, the equation of the circle is\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 + y^2 & = r^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = \\left({3\\sqrt2}\\right)^2 \\\\[5pt]\nx^2 + y^2 & = 18.\n\\end{align*}",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =  18$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 18$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =  72$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 72$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =  81$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 81$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x^2 + y^2 = 3 \\sqrt{2}$",
              "text": "$x^2 + y^2 = 3 \\sqrt{2}$"
            },

              "number": 5,
              "html": "$(x-3)^2 + (y-3)^2 =  36$",
              "text": "$(x-3)^2 + (y-3)^2 = 36$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {

            "html": "$x^2 + y^2 =  72$",

          "number": 1,

          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:30:37.000Z",

          "time_elapsed_ms": 29064,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:29",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality  $x^2 \\gt 16$ using a graphical method.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $x^2 \\gt 16$ using a graphical method.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 & \\gt 16 \\\\[3pt]\nx^2-16  & \\gt 0.\n}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-16 \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-16 &= 0\\\\[3pt]\nx^2-4^2 &= 0\\\\[3pt]\n(x-4)(x+4) &= 0\n}\n\\]\nSo the roots are $x= \\pm 4.$ </p>\n<p>Solving the inequality $x^2-16 \\gt 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-16$ is above the $x$-axis (where ${\\color{red}y \\gt 0}$).</p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (but it does not include them), as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-q-115074-e-0\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/q-115074-e-0\" /></div>\n\n<p>Therefore, the solution is $x \\in \\color{blue}(-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 & \\gt 16 \\\\[3pt]\nx^2-16 & \\gt 0.\n}\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-16 \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-16 &= 0\\\\[3pt]\nx^2-4^2 &= 0\\\\[3pt]\n(x-4)(x+4) &= 0\n}\n\\]\nSo the roots are $x= \\pm 4.$\nSolving the inequality $x^2-16 \\gt 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-16$ is above the $x$-axis (where ${\\color{red}y \\gt 0}$).\nSince the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (but it does not include them), as shown below.\nTherefore, the solution is $x \\in \\color{blue}(-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -8) \\cup (8, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -8) \\cup (8, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (8, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (8, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x \\in (16, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (16, \\infty)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty)$",
            "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -4) \\cup (4, \\infty)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 455885,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8, \nxmin = -10,\nxmax = 10,\nymin = -20,\nymax = 10,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-20.5:-4,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-16 };\n\\addplot [domain=-4:4,samples=200,color=black, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-16};\n\\addplot [domain=4:20.5,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-16};\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<                     -> , blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-8.5,0) -- (axis cs:8.5,0);\n\\draw[<                     - , blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-10,0) -- (axis cs:-3.92,0); \n\\draw[ ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:4.08,0) -- (axis cs:9.9,0); \n\n%Dots\n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A1) at (axis cs:-4,0) {}; \n\\coordinate [label={below left:{\\small $-4$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-4,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A2) at (axis cs:4,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below right:{\\small $4$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:4,0);\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-115074-e-0"
            }

        {
          "number": 2,
          "question_id": 72523,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5056,
          "example_title": "Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Graphical Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "E",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyEasy",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:31:06.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:31 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 26683,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:27",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality  $x^2 \\geq 4$ using a graphical method.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $x^2 \\geq 4$ using a graphical method.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 &\\geq 4 \\\\\nx^2-4  &\\geq 0.}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-4 \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-4 &= 0\\\\\nx^2-2^2 &= 0\\\\\n(x-2)(x+2) &= 0\n}\n\\]\nSo the roots are $x= \\pm 2.$ </p>\n<p>Solving the inequality $x^2-4 \\geq 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-4$ is above or intersects the $x$-axis (where ${\\color{red}y \\geq 0}$).</p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (including them), as shown below.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-q-72523-e-0\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/q-72523-e-0\" /></div>\n\n<p>Therefore, the solution is $x \\in \\color{blue}(-\\infty, -2] \\cup [2, \\infty).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "Bringing all the terms to the left-hand side, we have\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 &\\geq 4 \\\\\nx^2-4 &\\geq 0.}\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-4 \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-4 &= 0\\\\\nx^2-2^2 &= 0\\\\\n(x-2)(x+2) &= 0\n}\n\\]\nSo the roots are $x= \\pm 2.$\nSolving the inequality $x^2-4 \\geq 0$ is equivalent to finding the values of $x$ for which the parabola $y=x^2-4$ is above or intersects the $x$-axis (where ${\\color{red}y \\geq 0}$).\nSince the leading coefficient of the quadratic expression is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set lies outside the roots (including them), as shown below.\nTherefore, the solution is $x \\in \\color{blue}(-\\infty, -2] \\cup [2, \\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [2,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [2,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in [-2, 2]$",
              "text": "$x \\in [-2, 2]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-4] \\cup [4,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-4] \\cup [4,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x \\in [-4,4]$",
              "text": "$x \\in [-4,4]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x \\in [-2,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in [-2,\\infty)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [2,\\infty)$",
            "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty,-2] \\cup [2,\\infty)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 455874,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8, \nxmin = -7.5,\nxmax = 7.5,\nymin = -10,\nymax = 8,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-7.5:-2,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-4};\n\\addplot [domain=-2:2,samples=200,color=black, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-4};\n\\addplot [domain=2:7.5,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-4};\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<                -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-7.08,0) -- (axis cs:1.92,0);\n\\draw[<                - , blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-10.8,0) -- (axis cs:-1.98,0); \n\\draw[ ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0); \n\n%Dots\n\\node[circle,draw=blue,fill=blue,inner sep=1.5pt] (A1) at (axis cs:-2,0) {}; \n\\coordinate [label={above left:{\\small $-2$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-2.1,0);\n\\node[circle,draw=blue,fill=blue,inner sep=1.5pt] (A2) at (axis cs:2,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={above right:{\\small $2$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:2.1,0);\n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-72523-e-0"

        {
          "number": 3,
          "question_id": 115087,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5060,
          "example_title": "Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Absolute Value Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:33:39.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:33 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 30304,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:30",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality $x^2-15 \\lt 5$ using the absolute value method.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $x^2-15 \\lt 5$ using the absolute value method.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>Bringing the constant term to the right-hand side, we have\n\\begin{align*}\n\tx^2 -15& \\lt 5\\\\[3pt]\n\tx^2 & \\lt 20.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Then, taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 &\\lt 20\\\\[3pt]\n\\sqrt{x^2} &\\lt \\sqrt{20}\\\\[3pt]\n|x| & \\lt 2\\sqrt{5}.\n}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have\n\\[\n-2\\sqrt{5} \\lt x \\lt 2\\sqrt{5}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>The solution is $x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5).$</p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "Bringing the constant term to the right-hand side, we have\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2 -15& \\lt 5\\\\[3pt]\nx^2 & \\lt 20.\n\\end{align*}\nThen, taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2 &\\lt 20\\\\[3pt]\n\\sqrt{x^2} &\\lt \\sqrt{20}\\\\[3pt]\n|x| & \\lt 2\\sqrt{5}.\n}\n\\]\nNow, using the definition of absolute value, we must have\n\\[\n-2\\sqrt{5} \\lt x \\lt 2\\sqrt{5}.\n\\]\nThe solution is $x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty,2\\sqrt 5)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty,2\\sqrt 5)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -2\\sqrt 5) \\cup (2\\sqrt 5, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, -2\\sqrt 5) \\cup (2\\sqrt 5, \\infty)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5)$",
            "text": "$x \\in (-2\\sqrt 5,2\\sqrt 5)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",

        {
          "number": 4,
          "question_id": 30917,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5060,
          "example_title": "Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality Using the Absolute Value Method",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:34:29.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:34 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 47664,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:48",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality $x^2-1<     -\\sqrt{2}\\,$ using the absolute value method.</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $x^2-1< -\\sqrt{2}\\,$ using the absolute value method.",
          "explanation_html": "<p>Bringing the constant $-1$ to the right-hand side, we have</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\nx^2-1&<     -\\sqrt{2} \\\\\nx^2 &<     1-\\sqrt{2}.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Notice that $1-\\sqrt{2}<     0$ and that $x^2$, being a square, must be a positive number or zero. </p>\n<p>Hence, $x^2$ cannot be less than $1-\\sqrt{2}.$ </p>\n<p>Therefore, there is no solution. </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "Bringing the constant $-1$ to the right-hand side, we have\n\\begin{align*}\nx^2-1&< -\\sqrt{2} \\\\\nx^2 &< 1-\\sqrt{2}.\n\\end{align*}\nNotice that $1-\\sqrt{2}< 0$ and that $x^2$, being a square, must be a positive number or zero.\nHence, $x^2$ cannot be less than $1-\\sqrt{2}.$\nTherefore, there is no solution.",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "There is no solution",
              "text": "There is no solution"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$-2 <             x <             \\sqrt{2}$",
              "text": "$-2 < x < \\sqrt{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$-\\sqrt{2} <  x <  1$",
              "text": "$-\\sqrt{2} < x < 1$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$-\\sqrt{2} <             x <             \\sqrt{2}$",
              "text": "$-\\sqrt{2} < x < \\sqrt{2}$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x <         \\sqrt{2}$",
              "text": "$x < \\sqrt{2}$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "There is no solution",
            "text": "There is no solution",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [],
          "raw": {
            "answer_id": 12863354,
            "select_id": null,
            "question_format": "MC"
          }
        },
        {
          "number": 5,
          "question_id": 30918,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5059,
          "example_title": "Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality by Manipulating the Inequality First",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:35:20.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:35 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 23435,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:23",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve $6x^2<         9.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve $6x^2< 9.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s simplify the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n6x^2 &<         9 \\\\\nx^2 &<         \\dfrac{9}{6}\\\\\nx^2 &<         \\dfrac{3}{2}\\\\\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.</p>\n<hr>\n<p><em>Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation</em></p>\n<p>Taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\n\\sqrt{x^2} &<         \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\\\\n|x|&<         \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\n.}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have $-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}<        x<       \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}.$</p>\n<p>So, the solution is $x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right).$</p>\n<hr>\n<p><em>Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve</em></p>\n<p>Starting from $x^2 <         \\dfrac{3}{2}$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach</p>\n<p>\\[\nx^2-\\dfrac 32 <         0 .\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-\\dfrac{3}{2} \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-\\dfrac{3}{2} &= 0\\\\\nx^2-\\left(\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)^2 &= 0\\\\\n\\left(x-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)\\left(x+\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right) &= 0, \n}\n\\]\nhence $x= \\pm \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}.$</p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of $x^2-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}$   is positive, the parabola  opens upward.  Therefore, the solution set to the  given inequality ($y <       0$) lies between the roots (but it does not include them):</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-q-30918-e-0\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/q-30918-e-0\" /></div>\n\n<p>Therefore, the solution is $x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right).$   </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's simplify the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n6x^2 &< 9 \\\\\nx^2 &< \\dfrac{9}{6}\\\\\nx^2 &< \\dfrac{3}{2}\\\\\n\\end{align*}\nNow, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.\nMethod 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation\nTaking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\n\\sqrt{x^2} &< \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\\\\n|x|&< \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\n.}\n\\]\nNow, using the definition of absolute value, we must have $-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}< x< \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}.$\nSo, the solution is $x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right).$\nMethod 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve\nStarting from $x^2 < \\dfrac{3}{2}$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach\n\\[\nx^2-\\dfrac 32 < 0 .\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-\\dfrac{3}{2} \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-\\dfrac{3}{2} &= 0\\\\\nx^2-\\left(\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)^2 &= 0\\\\\n\\left(x-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)\\left(x+\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right) &= 0,\n}\n\\]\nhence $x= \\pm \\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}.$\nSince the leading coefficient of $x^2-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality ($y < 0$) lies between the roots (but it does not include them):\nTherefore, the solution is $x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": null,
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)$\n",
              "text": "$x\\in \\left(-\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}},\\sqrt{\\dfrac{3}{2}}\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": " $x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{9}{6}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{9}{6}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{9}{6}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{9}{6}, \\infty\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,

              "id": 455863,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -4,\nxmax = 4,\nymin = -2,\nymax = 4,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-7.5:-1.22,samples=200,color=black, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-sqrt(3/2) };\n\\addplot [domain=-1.22:1.22,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-sqrt(3/2)};\n\\addplot [domain=1.22:7.5,samples=200,color=black, line width=0.6pt]{x^2-sqrt(3/2)};\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<           -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-6.08,0) -- (axis cs: -1.22,0);\n\\draw[<         -> , blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-1.05,0) -- (axis cs:1.05,0);\n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:1.22.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n%Dots\n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A1) at (axis cs:-1.14,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below left:{\\small $-\\sqrt{\\frac{3}{2}}$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-1.2,0); \n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A2) at (axis cs:1.14,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below right:{\\small $\\sqrt{\\frac{3}{2}}$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:1.1,0); \n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

        {
          "number": 6,
          "question_id": 27568,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5059,
          "example_title": "Solving an Elementary Quadratic Inequality by Manipulating the Inequality First",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:35:37.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:35 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 12141,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:12",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve $4x^2> 9.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve $4x^2> 9.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s simplify the inequality:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n4x^2 &>9\\\\[3pt]\nx^2 &> \\dfrac{9}{4}\\\\\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.</p>\n<hr>\n<p><em>Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation</em></p>\n<p>Taking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get</p>\n<p>\\[\n\\eqalign{\n\\sqrt{x^2} &> \\sqrt{ \\dfrac{9}{4}}\\\\\n|x| &>  \\dfrac{3}{2}.\\\\\n}\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, using the definition of absolute value, we must have </p>\n<p>\\[\nx<    -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\quad\\textrm{or}\\quad  x>\\dfrac{3}{2}.\n\\]</p>\n<p>So, the solution is $x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup\\left ( \\dfrac{3}{2}, +\\infty\\right).$</p>\n<hr>\n<p><em>Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve</em></p>\n<p>Starting from $x^2>  \\dfrac{9}{4} $ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach</p>\n<p>\\[\nx^2- \\dfrac{9}{4} >   0 .\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-\\dfrac{9}{4} \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-\\dfrac{9}{4} &= 0\\\\\nx^2-\\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)^2 &= 0\\\\\n\\left(x-\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)\\left(x+\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) &= 0,\n}\n\\]\nso the roots are  $x= \\pm \\dfrac{3}{2}.$ </p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of $x^2-\\dfrac{9}{4}$   is positive, the parabola opens upward. Hence, the solution set to the given inequality ($y > 0$) lies outside the roots and does not include them:</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-q-27568-e-0\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/q-27568-e-0\" /></div> \n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n<p>Therefore, the solution is $x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right).$    </p>\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's simplify the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n4x^2 &>9\\\\[3pt]\nx^2 &> \\dfrac{9}{4}\\\\\n\\end{align*}\nNow, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.\nMethod 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Equation\nTaking the square root of both sides of the inequality, we get\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\n\\sqrt{x^2} &> \\sqrt{ \\dfrac{9}{4}}\\\\\n|x| &> \\dfrac{3}{2}.\\\\\n}\n\\]\nNow, using the definition of absolute value, we must have\n\\[\nx< -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\quad\\textrm{or}\\quad x>\\dfrac{3}{2}.\n\\]\nSo, the solution is $x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup\\left ( \\dfrac{3}{2}, +\\infty\\right).$\nMethod 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve\nStarting from $x^2> \\dfrac{9}{4} $ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach\n\\[\nx^2- \\dfrac{9}{4} > 0 .\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = x^2-\\dfrac{9}{4} \\, $ and find its roots:\n\\[\n\\eqalign{\nx^2-\\dfrac{9}{4} &= 0\\\\\nx^2-\\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)^2 &= 0\\\\\n\\left(x-\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)\\left(x+\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) &= 0,\n}\n\\]\nso the roots are $x= \\pm \\dfrac{3}{2}.$\nSince the leading coefficient of $x^2-\\dfrac{9}{4}$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. Hence, the solution set to the given inequality ($y > 0$) lies outside the roots and does not include them:\nTherefore, the solution is $x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{9}{4}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{9}{4}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{9}{4}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{9}{4}, \\infty\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": " $x \\in \\left(\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)\\cap \\left(-\\dfrac{9}{2}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right)\\cap \\left(-\\dfrac{9}{2}, \\infty\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)\\cup \\left(\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{1}{2}\\right)\\cup \\left(\\dfrac{1}{2}, \\infty\\right)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "  $x \\in  \\left(\\dfrac{9}{4}, \\infty\\right)$",
              "text": "$x \\in \\left(\\dfrac{9}{4}, \\infty\\right)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right)$",
            "text": "$x \\in \\left(-\\infty, -\\dfrac{3}{2}\\right) \\cup \\left(\\dfrac{3}{2}, \\infty\\right)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 455849,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\naxis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -5,\nxmax = 5,\nymin = -5,\nymax = 5,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-7.5:-1.5,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.8pt]{x^2-2.25 };\n\\addplot [domain=-1.5:1.5,samples=200,color=black, line width=0.8pt]{x^2-2.25};\n\\addplot [domain=1.5:7.5,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.8pt]{x^2-2.25};\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<            -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-7.08,0) -- (axis cs:1.92,0);\n\\draw[<            - , blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-6,0) -- (axis cs:-1.58,0);\n\\draw[ ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:1.58,0) -- (axis cs:6,0);\n\n%Dots\n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A1) at (axis cs:-1.5,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below left:{\\small $-\\frac{3}{2}$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-1.4,0); \n\\node[circle,draw=red,fill=white,inner sep=1.5pt] (A2) at (axis cs:1.5,0) {};\n\\coordinate [label={below right:{\\small $\\frac{3}{2}$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:1.4,0);  \n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-27568-e-0"

        {
          "number": 7,
          "question_id": 115270,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5058,
          "example_title": "Elementary Quadratic Inequalities with No Solutions or All Real Solutions",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:37:18.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:37 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 17969,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:18",
          "result": "correct",
          "correct": true,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality $5x^2+2x \\gt  2x-3.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $5x^2+2x \\gt 2x-3.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s simplify the inequality:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n5x^2+2x&> 2x-3\\\\[3pt]\n5x^2+2x-2x &> 2x-3-2x\\\\[3pt]\n5x^2&> -3.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.</p>\n<p><strong>Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality</strong></p>\n<p>We know that the square of <em>any</em> real number is greater than or equal to zero.</p>\n<p>So, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$ </p>\n<p><strong>Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve</strong></p>\n<p>Starting from $5x^2 \\gt -3$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach</p>\n<p>\\[\n5x^2+3 \\gt 0.\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = 5x^2+3. $ :</p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of $ 5x^2+3$   is positive, the parabola opens upward.  The desired inequality $(y \\gt 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-344e3f94e7ecba95940446ea727791d0\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/344e3f94e7ecba95940446ea727791d0\" /></div>\n\n\n\n\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's simplify the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n5x^2+2x&> 2x-3\\\\[3pt]\n5x^2+2x-2x &> 2x-3-2x\\\\[3pt]\n5x^2&> -3.\n\\end{align*}\nNow, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.\nMethod 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality\nWe know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero.\nSo, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$\nMethod 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve\nStarting from $5x^2 \\gt -3$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach\n\\[\n5x^2+3 \\gt 0.\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $\\, y = 5x^2+3. $ :\nSince the leading coefficient of $ 5x^2+3$ is positive, the parabola opens upward. The desired inequality $(y \\gt 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": "$x \\in (-3, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-3, \\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, 3)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, 3)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": "$x \\in (-3, 3)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (-3, 3)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "$x \\in (3, \\infty)$",
              "text": "$x \\in (3, \\infty)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 1,
            "html": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$",
            "text": "$x \\in (-\\infty, \\infty)$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 342623,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -4,\nxmax = 4,\nymin = -5,\nymax = 20,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n \n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-4:6,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.8pt]{5*x^2+3 };   \n\n\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<               -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-6.08,0) -- (axis cs: -2.02,0);\n\\draw[<             ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-4.0,0) -- (axis cs:4.0,0); \n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n\n\\end{axis} \n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "344e3f94e7ecba95940446ea727791d0"
            },
            {
              "id": 455900,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -4,\nxmax = 4,\nymin = -5,\nymax = 20,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n \n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-4:6,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.8pt]{5*x^2+3 };   \n\n\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<               -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-6.08,0) -- (axis cs: -2.02,0);\n\\draw[<             ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-4.0,0) -- (axis cs:4.0,0); \n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n\n\\end{axis} \n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
              "fileId": "q-115270-e-0"
            }

        {
          "number": 8,
          "question_id": 70274,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5058,
          "example_title": "Elementary Quadratic Inequalities with No Solutions or All Real Solutions",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "H",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyHard",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:38:21.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:38 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 59329,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:59",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality $2x^2-10\\leq 4x^2.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $2x^2-10\\leq 4x^2.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>First, let&#39;s simplify the inequality:</p>\n<p>\\begin{align*}\n2x^2-10&\\leq 4x^2 \\\\\n2x^2-4x^2 &\\leq 10\\\\\n-2x^2 &\\leq 10\\\\\nx^2&\\geq -\\dfrac{ 10}{2}\\\\\nx^2&\\geq -5.\n\\end{align*}</p>\n<p>Now, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.</p>\n<p><strong>Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality</strong></p>\n<p>We know that the square of <em>any</em> real number is greater than or equal to zero.</p>\n<p>So, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$ </p>\n<p><strong>Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve</strong></p>\n<p>Starting from $-2x^2 \\leq 10$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach</p>\n<p>\\[\n-2x^2-10 \\leq 0 .\n\\]</p>\n<p>Now, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $y = -2x^2-10$:</p>\n<p>Since the leading coefficient of $ -2x^2-10$   is negative, the parabola opens downward (like $\\frown$). The desired inequality $(y\\leq 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-9955795f512eff0b3c3cd4287ae500f4\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/9955795f512eff0b3c3cd4287ae500f4\" /></div>\n\n\n\n\n\n\n",
          "explanation_text": "First, let's simplify the inequality:\n\\begin{align*}\n2x^2-10&\\leq 4x^2 \\\\\n2x^2-4x^2 &\\leq 10\\\\\n-2x^2 &\\leq 10\\\\\nx^2&\\geq -\\dfrac{ 10}{2}\\\\\nx^2&\\geq -5.\n\\end{align*}\nNow, we can use either of two methods to solve the inequality.\nMethod 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality\nWe know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero.\nSo, the inequality is true for every real number. Therefore the solution is $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$\nMethod 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve\nStarting from $-2x^2 \\leq 10$ and moving all terms to the left-hand side, we reach\n\\[\n-2x^2-10 \\leq 0 .\n\\]\nNow, we consider the corresponding parabola given by $y = -2x^2-10$:\nSince the leading coefficient of $ -2x^2-10$ is negative, the parabola opens downward (like $\\frown$). The desired inequality $(y\\leq 0)$ is always satisfied. Therefore, the solution set to the given inequality includes all real numbers. That is, $x \\in (-\\infty,\\infty).$",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": " $x\\in(-\\infty,\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in(-\\infty,\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": " $x\\in[-\\sqrt{5},\\sqrt{5}]$",
              "text": "$x\\in[-\\sqrt{5},\\sqrt{5}]$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": " $x\\in (-\\infty,-{5}]$",

              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -6,\nxmax = 6,\nymin = -25,\nymax = 6,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n \n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-6:6,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{-2*x^2-10 };  \n\n\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<                -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-6.08,0) -- (axis cs: -2.02,0);\n\\draw[<              ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-6.0,0) -- (axis cs:6.02,0); \n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n\n\\end{axis} \n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

              "id": 455872,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -6,\nxmax = 6,\nymin = -25,\nymax = 6,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n \n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-6:6,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.6pt]{-2*x^2-10 };  \n\n\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<                -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-6.08,0) -- (axis cs: -2.02,0);\n\\draw[<              ->, blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-6.0,0) -- (axis cs:6.02,0); \n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n\n\\end{axis} \n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",

        {
          "number": 9,
          "question_id": 70275,
          "task_id": 2829175,
          "task_type": "Lesson",
          "topic_id": 1495,
          "topic_name": "Solving Elementary Quadratic Inequalities",
          "example_id": 5058,
          "example_title": "Elementary Quadratic Inequalities with No Solutions or All Real Solutions",
          "format": "MC",
          "difficulty": "M",
          "difficulty_class": "difficultyModerate",
          "created_at": "2025-01-12T20:38:42.000Z",
          "created_display": "Sun, Jan 12th @ 8:38 PM",
          "time_elapsed_ms": 15151,
          "time_elapsed_display": "0:15",
          "result": "incorrect",
          "correct": false,
          "question_html": "<p>Solve the inequality $x^2<     -7.$</p>\n",
          "question_text": "Solve the inequality $x^2< -7.$",
          "explanation_html": "<p>We can solve this inequality using either of the two methods described below.</p>\n<p><strong>Method 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality</strong></p>\n<p>We know that the square of <em>any</em> real number is greater than or equal to zero. Therefore, it is impossible for the square of a real number to be smaller than $-7.$</p>\n<p>So, the inequality $x^2 <      -7$ has no solutions.</p>\n<p><strong>Method 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve</strong></p>\n<p>Let&#39;s bring all the terms to the left-hand side,\n\\[\nx^2+7 <      0,\n\\]\nand then plot the parabola $y=x^2+7.$ The desired inequality ($y<     0$) is never satisfied, so there are no solutions.</p>\n<div id=\"graphic-561a6f8ad1b6f4c89634bd2c6e4f0d66\" class=\"graphic\"><img src=\"/graphics/561a6f8ad1b6f4c89634bd2c6e4f0d66\" /></div> \n",
          "explanation_text": "We can solve this inequality using either of the two methods described below.\nMethod 1 - Solving an Absolute Value Inequality\nWe know that the square of any real number is greater than or equal to zero. Therefore, it is impossible for the square of a real number to be smaller than $-7.$\nSo, the inequality $x^2 < -7$ has no solutions.\nMethod 2 - Picturing a Quadratic Curve\nLet's bring all the terms to the left-hand side,\n\\[\nx^2+7 < 0,\n\\]\nand then plot the parabola $y=x^2+7.$ The desired inequality ($y< 0$) is never satisfied, so there are no solutions.",
          "free_answer_choice_html": " ",
          "free_answer_choice_text": "",
          "choices": [
            {
              "number": 1,
              "html": " The inequality has no solution",
              "text": "The inequality has no solution"
            },
            {
              "number": 2,
              "html": " $x\\in (-{7},{7})$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-{7},{7})$"
            },
            {
              "number": 3,
              "html": " $x\\in (-\\sqrt{7},\\sqrt{7})$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\sqrt{7},\\sqrt{7})$"
            },
            {
              "number": 4,
              "html": " $x\\in (-\\infty,-\\sqrt{7})\\cup (\\sqrt{7},\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,-\\sqrt{7})\\cup (\\sqrt{7},\\infty)$"
            },
            {
              "number": 5,
              "html": "  $x\\in (-\\infty,-{7})\\cup ({7},\\infty)$",
              "text": "$x\\in (-\\infty,-{7})\\cup ({7},\\infty)$"
            }
          ],
          "student_answer": {
            "selected_choice": 3,
            "html": " $x\\in (-\\sqrt{7},\\sqrt{7})$",
            "text": "$x\\in (-\\sqrt{7},\\sqrt{7})$",
            "free_response": [],
            "raw_choices": null
          },
          "hints": [],
          "hint_status": "No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.",
          "graphics": [
            {
              "id": 342580,
              "tex": "\\documentclass{article}\n\\usepackage{pgfplots}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n%\\usetikzlibrary{arrows} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usetikzlibrary{intersections} %% Can be useful, not essential\n%\\usepgfplotslibrary{fillbetween} %% Can be useful, not essential\n\\begin{document}\n\\pagestyle{empty} %% IMPORTANT\n\\begin{tikzpicture}\n\\begin{axis}[\n%trig format plots=rad, %% Ensures radians for trig functions\ntick style={draw=none},\ntick label style={font=\\footnotesize},\nlabel style={font=\\small},\nlegend style={font=\\footnotesize},\ntick style={draw=none},\naxis line style = thick,\naxis y line = center,\naxis x line = middle,\n%axis equal,\n%grid = both,\n%scale= 0.8,\nxmin = -8,\nxmax = 8,\nymin = -4,\nymax = 16,\nyticklabels={,,},\nxticklabels={,,},\nxlabel = $x$,\nylabel = {$y$},\nxlabel style={below right},\nylabel style={above left}\n]\n\n%Graphic:\n\\addplot [domain=-8:8,samples=200,color=red, line width=0.8pt]{x^2+7};\n\n% Arrows\n%\\draw[<                   -> , blue, line width=2.3pt] (axis cs:-7.08,0) -- (axis cs:1.92,0);\n%\\draw[<              -> ,  blue, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:-8,0) -- (axis cs:8,0);\n%\\draw[ ->, red, line width=1.5pt] (axis cs:2.08,0) -- (axis cs:10.8,0);\n\n\n%\\node[circle,draw=blue,fill=blue,inner sep=1.5pt] (A1) at (axis cs:-4,0) {}; \n%\\coordinate [label={above left:{\\small $-4$}}] (A1) at (axis cs:-4,0);\n%\\node[circle,draw=blue,fill=blue,inner sep=1.5pt] (A2) at (axis cs:4,0) {};\n%\\coordinate [label={above right:{\\small $4$}}] (A2) at (axis cs:4,0); \n\n\\end{axis}\n\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n\\end{document}",
